1-16.q: All sales and purchases for the year at Ross Corporation are credit transactions. Ross uses a perpetual inventory system and shipped goods that were correctly excluded from ending inventory. However, in error, the sale was not recorded. Which one of the following statements is correct?
o: Accounts receivable was understated, inventory was overstated, sales were understated, and cost of goods sold was overstated.
Accounts receivable was understated, inventory was not affected, sales were understated, and cost of goods sold was not affected.
Accounts receivable was understated, inventory was not affected, sales were understated, and cost of goods sold was understated.
Accounts receivable was not affected, inventory was not affected, sales were understated, and cost of goods sold was understated.
1-16.e: Because the goods were not included in ending inventory, inventory is not affected. Also, because the inventory was correctly recorded, cost of goods sold was not affected by the rror. However, by failing to record the sale, both accounts receivable and sales revenue were understated.
wrongWhy: Inventory and cost of goods sold were not overstated because the items sold were excluded from ending inventory.
Cost of goods sold was not understated because the items sold were excluded from ending inventory.

Accounts receivable was affected because the sale was not recorded. Cost of goods sold was not understated because the items sold were excluded from ending inventory. Ci PART SERTION A. etna nancial aqporing 220 Q 67 ay

1-16.q: The following inventory valuation errors have been discovered for Knox Corporation. The year 1 year-end inventory was overstated by $23,000. The year 2 year-end inventory was understated by $61,000. The year 3 year-end inventory was understated by $17,000. The reported income before taxes for Knox was Year 1 - $138,000 Year 2 - $254,000 Year 3 - $168,000 Reported income before taxes for year 1, year 2, and year 3, respectively, should have been
1-16.e: Though this is a three year question, we can solve it in the same manner we would a one year question. This is easiest to do if we create an example that first has numbers that the company used and then we make adjustments for the numbers that the company should have had. In this question it is a little bit more difficult because of the fact that we are given an income figure, but we can do it as follows: Year1 Year2 Year3 Beginning inventory 200 100 100 Purchases 300 600 500 Ending inventory 100 100 200 Cost of sales 400 600 400 Net income 138 254 168 Now, we can make the corrections to the ending inventory balances and see what income should have been. Year1 Year2 Year 3 Beginning inventory 200 77 161 Purchases 300 600 500 PART SERTION A. aetna nancial gporin 220 2 188 4 ve 10:44 Oe, CMA Part 1 — Test Bank — HOCK international MA PART 1 SECTION (A ) External Financail Reporting ***** Inventory Count, Errors, and Valuation 14 q 14 Ending inventory 77 161 217 Cost of sales 423 516 444 Net income 115 338 124 The question could also be answered by adjusting the reported income before taxes, as follows: Year 1: Income before taxes was reported as $138,000 and ending inventory was overstated by $23,000. An overstatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is understated and gross profit that is overstated, so corrected income before taxes was $138,000 — $23,000 = $115,000. Year 2: Beginning inventory was overstated by $23,000 due to the error in Year 1's ending inventory. An overstatement of beginning inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $23,000 due to the Year 1 error. Year 2's income before taxes was reported as $254,000 and ending inventory was understated by $61,000. An understatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $61,000 due to the Year 2 error. Combining both errors, the corrected income before taxes was $254,000 + $23,000 + $61,000 = i $338,000. Year 3: Beginning inventory was understated by $61,000 due to the error in Year 2's ending inventory. An understatement of beginning inventory leads to COGS that is understated and gross profit that is overstated, so income before taxes was overstated by $61,000 due to the Year 2 error. Year 3's income before tax was reported as $168,000 and ending inventory was understated by $17,000. An understatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $17,000 due to the Year 3 error. Combining both errors, the corrected income before taxes was $168,000 — $61,000 + $17,000 = $124,000.
wrongWhy: This answer results from incorrectly treating an overstated ending inventory as causing understated gross profit in the year of the error and overstated gross profit in the following year; and treating understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit in the year of the error and understated gross profit in the following year. The correct calculations are exactly opposite (replace all the additions with subtractions and all the subtractions with additions).

This answer results from incorrectly treating Year 3's understated beginning inventory as causing understated gross profit and incorrectly treating Year 3's understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit. The correct calculations for Year 3 are the opposite: an understated beginning inventory causes overstated gross profit and an understated ending inventory causes understated gross profit.

This answer results from incorrectly treating Year 1's overstated ending inventory as causing understated gross profit. An overstated ending inventory causes overstated gross profit. The answer also results from incorrectly treating Year 3's understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit. An understated ending inventory causes understated gross profit. Ci

1-16.q: This information concerns items in Wilson's inventory. Cameras Lenses Tripods Historical cost per unit $210.00 $110.00 $53.00 Selling price per unit 217.00 145.00 73.75 Cost to distribute per unit 19.00 8.00 2.50 Current replacement cost per unit 203.00 109.00 51.00 Normal profit margin per unit 32.00 29.00 21.25 Assume that Wilson uses the LIFO cost flow assumption to value its inventory and determine its cost of goods sold. The amount that should be used to value the tripods is
1-16.e: Since Wilson uses the LIFO cost flow assumption, its inventory is valued at the lower of cost or market. In the calculation of the lower of cost or market, the designated market value used must fall within a range. The upper limit of the range, the ceiling, is the net realizable value of the inventory. The floor is the net realizable value minus a normal profit margin. The designated market value is the middle value of three numbers, subject to the ceiling and the floor limitations. The three numbers and the way they are calculated are: . the ceiling, or net realizable value (sales price minus the costs to complete and sell), . the current replacement cost, and . the floor (the ceiling or net realizable value minus a normal profit margin). Using the numbers given for the tripods, the net realizable value or the ceiling for the designated market value is $71.25 ($73.75 selling price minus $2.50 costs to sell), the replacement value is $51.00, and the floor is $50.00 (the net realizable value or ceiling of $71.25 minus a normal profit margin of $21.25). The middle value of these three numbers, which is the designated market value, is $51.00, the replacement cost. The designated market value is then compared to the historical cost and the lower of the two amounts is used to value the inventory. Since the designated market value ($51.00) is lower than the historical cost ($53.00), the inventory will be valued at its designated market value of $51.00.

1-16.q: A multinational company maintains its financial records under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP. Last year, the company determined its inventory with a carrying amount of $500,000 was impaired because demand for its product collapsed when a competitor launched a new product with innovative features. As a result, the company wrote down its inventory to $0. This year, however, government authorities unexpectedly announced that the competitor’s product was defective and the product was removed from the market. As a result, the company’s products were again in demand and the company estimated their net realizable value to be $750,000 at the end of the current quarter. How should the company record this new development in the current quarter?
o: Under IFRS, 750,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $0 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $500,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $750,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $750,000 write-up of the inventory.
1-16.e: Under IFRS, previous write-downs of inventory may be recovered up to the original cost of the inventory. Gains cannot be recognized on appreciated inventory, but previous losses can be reversed. Under U.S. GAAP, inventory recoveries in value may be recorded only if they occur within the same fiscal year as the write-down occurred. Inventory write-downs reported on an annual financial statement may not be restored in a later annual period. Since the recovery of value occurred in a different fiscal year from the write- down, the inventory may not be written back up.
wrongWhy: Under IFRS, previous write-downs of inventory may be recovered up to the original cost of the inventory. Gains cannot be recognized on appreciated inventory, but previous losses can be reversed. Therefore, the inventory cannot be written up to 750,000 under IFRS because its carrying value before the write-down was only $500,000.
Under IFRS, previous write-downs of inventory may be recovered up to the original cost of the inventory.

Under IFRS, previous write-downs of inventory may be recovered up to the original cost of the inventory. Gains cannot be recognized on appreciated inventory, but previous losses can be reversed. Therefore, the inventory cannot be written up to 750,000 under IFRS because its carrying value before the write-down was only $500,000. Under U.S. GAAP, inventory recoveries in value may be recorded only if they occur within the same fiscal year as the write-down occurred. Inventory write-downs reported on an annual financial statement may not be restored in a later annual period. Since the recovery of value occurred in a different fiscal year from the write- down, the inventory may not be written back up. /2

1-17.q: Unrealized gains on Trading securities are reported in:
o: OCI — other comprehensive income
Net Income on the income statement
Not reported until sold
Retained earnings directly
1-17.e: Trading securities: reported at Fair Value with all unrealized gains/losses flowing through Net Income each period. Trading securities are held for short-term profit, so all FV changes are included in current earnings.

1-17.q: Unrealized gains on Available-For-Sale (AFS) debt securities are reported in:
o: Net Income
OCI — Other Comprehensive Income (accumulated in Aoci on BS)
Not reported until sold
Retained earnings directly
1-17.e: AFS securities: reported at Fair Value, but unrealized gains/losses go to OCI (accumulated in Aoci in equity section of BS). They are not recognized in net income until the securities are Sold (reclassification adjustment at sale).

1-17.q: Which type of investment can be classified as Held-to-Maturity (HTM)?
o: Equity securities — stocks
Mutual fund investments
Debt securities only — bonds and notes
Any investment with maturity date
1-17.e: Only Debt Securities (bonds, notes with a fixed maturity date) can be classified as HTM. Equity securities (stocks) do Not have a maturity date and Cannot be classified as HTM. Under ASC 321, equity securities are always at fair value through net income.

1-17.q: HTM securities are measured at:
o: Fair value with changes in NI
Fair value with changes in OCI
Amortized cost — no unrealized G/L reported
Lower of cost or Nrv
1-17.e: HTM securities are measured at Amortized Cost. Because the company intends and is able to hold them to maturity (collecting face value at maturity), unrealized fair value fluctuations are irrelevant. No unrealized gain/loss is reported.

1-17.q: When an AFS security is Sold, the accumulated unrealized gain in Aoci is:
o: Reversed and forgotten
Reclassified from Aoci to Net Income (reclassification adjustment)
Retained in Aoci permanently
Added to retained earnings
1-17.e: When an AFS security is sold, the accumulated unrealized gain/loss in Aoci is Reclassified to Net Income via a reclassification adjustment. This prevents double-counting — the gain was already in OCI; now it moves to NI upon realization.

1-17.q: A bond purchased at a Premium means:
o: Purchase price < face value; generates a discount
Purchase price > face value; excess amortized as reduction to interest income
The bond has a high credit rating
Interest payments exceed the coupon rate
1-17.e: Bond at Premium: purchase price > face value. The premium is Amortized over the bond's life, Reducing interest income below the coupon rate each period. At maturity, the book value equals face value (premium fully amortized).

1-17.q: The Cecl model (Current Expected Credit Losses) requires:
o: Recognizing credit losses only when probable
Estimating Lifetime expected losses on held debt instruments at Acquisition — forward-looking
Only applying to trading securities
Recognizing losses only at maturity
1-17.e: Cecl (ASC 326): forward-looking model requiring estimation of Lifetime expected credit losses at the time of Acquisition (day 1). This replaces the 'incurred loss' model where losses were recognized only when probable. Cecl is more conservative and timely.

1-17.q: Trading securities are reported on the balance sheet at:
o: Amortized cost
Lower of cost or market
Fair value, with unrealized gains/losses in net income
Historical cost
1-17.e: Trading securities are always measured at Fair Value. All unrealized gains and losses flow through Net Income each period. The intent is short-term profit — so fair value changes are immediately income-relevant.

1-17.q: Interest income on Held-to-Maturity bonds is recognized using:
o: The cash basis — only when received
The straight-line method only
The effective interest method (preferred) or straight-line
Only when the bond matures
1-17.e: Interest income on HTM (and AFS) bonds is accrued using the Effective Interest Method (the theoretically correct approach). Straight-line is allowed if the difference is not material. Both are GAAP-acceptable.

1-17.q: A bond is purchased at a Premium (above par). Under the effective interest method for an HTM bond:
o: The premium is amortized, increasing interest income above the coupon
The premium is amortized, Reducing interest income below the coupon rate
The premium is carried unchanged until maturity
The premium is immediately expensed
1-17.e: When a bond is purchased at a Premium, the yield (effective rate) is Less than the coupon rate. As the premium is amortized, the carrying value Decreases toward par, and interest income recognized = carrying value × effective rate (less than cash received).

1-17.q: Under the effective interest method, amortization of a bond Discount:
o: Reduces the carrying value of the bond
Increases the carrying value toward par value
Has no effect on the carrying value
Reduces interest income below the coupon rate
1-17.e: Bond Discount amortization: carrying value Increases toward par with each period. Interest income recognized = carrying value × effective rate (Greater than cash coupon), and the difference = discount amortized (added to carrying value).

1-17.q: A company that Frequently sells HTM securities before maturity may be:
o: Allowed to continue classifying bonds as HTM
Required to reclassify all HTM securities to AFS and may be barred from HTM classification for 2 years
Fined by the FASB
Required to reclassify to trading securities immediately
1-17.e: HTM requires Positive Intent And Ability to hold to maturity. Frequent early sales Taint the portfolio — the company may be required to reclassify remaining HTM securities to AFS and may be barred from using HTM for a two-year 'tainting period.'

1-17.q: When an AFS debt security is Transferred to HTM, the unrealized gain or loss at the transfer date is:
o: Reversed immediately through income
Recognized in income at the transfer date
Remains in Aoci and is amortized over the remaining life of the security
Eliminated from both Aoci and the carrying value
1-17.e: On transfer from AFS to HTM, the unrealized gain or loss stays in Aoci but is Amortized out of Aoci into interest income over the remaining life of the security — offsetting the HTM amortization.

1-17.q: Under the Cecl model, credit loss allowances on HTM debt securities are based on:
o: Losses already incurred (incurred loss model)
Expected credit losses over the life of the instrument, recognized immediately
Fair value changes in the security
Management discretion at year-end
1-17.e: Cecl (Current Expected Credit Losses — ASC 326) replaced the incurred loss model with an Expected loss model. Companies must recognize All expected credit losses over the remaining life of the instrument on Day One, not wait until a loss is probable.

1-17.q: Under ASC 321, an equity security Without significant influence has an unrealized loss at period-end. This loss is reported in:
o: Net Income — all fair value changes on equity securities flow through net income each period
Other comprehensive income (Aoci) — same as AFS debt securities
Retained earnings directly, bypassing the income statement
Notes only — unrealized losses are not recognized until the security is sold
1-17.e: Under ASC 321 (effective 2018, post-ASU 2016-01), All equity securities with a readily determinable fair value are measured at Fair Value with unrealized gains And losses recognized in Net Income each period. The Available-for-Sale (AFS) classification no longer applies to equity securities — it was eliminated by ASU 2016-01. This is a key difference from AFS Debt securities, where unrealized changes still go to OCI. Exception: equity securities without a readily determinable FV may use the measurement alternative (cost minus impairment ± observable price changes).

1-17.q: The Fair Value Option under ASC 825 allows companies to:
o: Elect to measure certain financial instruments at fair value, with changes in income
Apply fair value to All assets on the balance sheet
Avoid recording unrealized losses by electing fair value
Use fair value only for liabilities, not assets
1-17.e: The fair value option is an Election — companies may choose to measure eligible financial instruments (loans, debt, receivables) at fair value with changes recognized in net income. Once elected, the election is generally irrevocable.

1-17.q: A company reclassifies a bond from HTM to AFS due to a change in circumstances. The bond's fair value is $105,000 and amortized cost is $98,000. At reclassification:
o: Record a $7,000 gain in net income
Record a $7,000 unrealized gain in Aoci
No entry needed — HTM bonds cannot be reclassified
Write down the bond to $98,000
1-17.e: On reclassification from HTM to AFS, the bond is remeasured to Fair Value. The unrealized gain of $7,000 is recognized in Other Comprehensive Income (Aoci), not net income. The new carrying value = $105,000.

1-17.q: Effective interest rate on a bond equals:
o: The stated coupon rate printed on the bond
The market rate of interest at the time the bond was issued / purchased
The coupon rate divided by par value
The rate that makes future cash flows equal to par value
1-17.e: The effective interest rate = the Market Rate (yield) when the bond was issued. It is the rate that discounts the bond's future cash flows (coupons + principal) to the Purchase Price (present value). Coupon rate is stated; effective rate reflects market conditions.

1-17.q: Under IFRS 9, financial assets are classified based on:
o: Management's intent (trading, HTM, AFS) — same as US GAAP
Business model for managing assets and contractual cash flow characteristics
The asset's maturity and credit rating
Whether the asset is equity or debt only
1-17.e: IFRS 9 uses Two criteria: (1) Business Model — is it held to collect, hold and sell, or trade? (2) Cash Flow Characteristics — does it give rise to solely payments of principal and interest (Sppi test)? This differs from the intent-based categories under US GAAP.

1-17.q: An unrealized loss on an AFS Debt security appears in Aoci. When the security is Sold at a loss, the amount previously in Aoci is:
o: Reversed to zero with no income effect
Reclassified out of Aoci into net income (loss is realized)
Remains in Aoci permanently
Added to the cost basis of the replacement security
1-17.e: When an AFS debt security is Sold, accumulated unrealized gains or losses in Aoci are Reclassified (recycled) into net income. The unrealized loss becomes a Realized loss recognized in income in the period of sale.

1-18.q: Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock at a historical cost of $300,000. Supreme Table recently reported their earnings for the prior year. Best Billiard's proportional share of Supreme Table's prior year net income was $10,000. Best Billiard also received $15,000 in dividends from Supreme Table in the prior year. Best Billiard uses the quity method as the accounting treatment for this investment. Based on the information presented, the proper presentation of this investment would result in Best Billiard reporting
o: a decrease in the book value of their investment in Supreme Table.
consolidated financial statements with Supreme Table.
their investment in Supreme Table at the original cost.
an increase in the book value of their investment in Supreme Table.
1-18.e: Equity securities where the investor exercises significant influence over the investee, whether publicly or privately held, are accounted for under the equity method. "Significant influence” is usually assumed when the investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee's voting stock. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the equity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee's income. In this case, that is given to us as $10,000. Additionally, the investment account is reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. In this question that amount is $15,000. The $10,000 increase in the investment account and the $15,000 decrease in the investment account lead to an overall decrease in the investment account of $5,000.
wrongWhy: The investment in Supreme Table does not require consolidation. Equity securities where the investor exercises significant influence over the investee, whether publicly or privately held, are accounted for under the equity method. "Significant influence" is usually assumed when the investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee’s voting stock. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the quity method. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. ——_______Explanation for C2 Ci ve 10:52 2024/8/15 CMA Part.1 — Test Bank — HOCK international The investment in Supreme Table should not be carried at the original cost. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the equity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee’s income and reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The events that are described in the question would not cause the book value of Best Billiard's investment in Supreme Table to increase during the period. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the equity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee's income and reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. PART 1 SECTION as Fademal Financial Rgporting 330 Q 120 Ci /2

1-18.q: Acompany should apply the equity method of accounting for an investment whenever it can xercise significant influence over the investee. Usually, the minimum level of ownership at which an investor can exercise significant influence is
o: 25% ownership.
10% ownership.
20% ownership.
50% ownership.
1-18.e: Usually a 20% ownership share indicates significant influence. However, the share ownership by itself does not determine whether significant influence exists, it is merely an indicator. Other factors such as other owners, board membership and past history also need to be considered.
wrongWhy: This is not the minimum percentage of ownership at which an investor is usually considered to be able to exercise significant influence. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This is not the minimum percentage of ownership at which an investor is usually considered to be able to exercise significant influence. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

1-18.q: On January 1, Boggs, Inc. paid $700,000 for 100,000 shares of Mattly Corporation representing 30% of Mattly's outstanding common stock. The following computation was made by Boggs. Purchase price: $700,000 % equity in fair value of Mattly's net assets: $500,000 Excess cost over fair value: $200,000 The excess cost over fair value was attributed to goodwill. Mattly reported net income for the year ended December 31 of $300,000. Mattly Corporation had paid cash dividends of $100,000 on July 1. If Boggs, Inc. exercised significant influence over Mattly Corporation and properly accounted for the long-term investment under the equity method, the amount of net investment revenue Boggs should report from its investment in Mattly would be:
1-18.e: Because the equity method is used, Boggs should report as investment income each period their percentage of Mattly's income. Since Mattly had $300,000 of income and Boggs owns % of Mattly, their share of the income is $90,000.
wrongWhy: This is the amount of income that would be recognized under the fair value method. Under the fair value method, income is recognized as dividends are received. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
Because the equity method is used, Boggs should report as investment income each period their percentage of Mattly's income. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

consolidated, unless the noncontrolling interest in the subsidiary is very large.
consolidated, unless the subsidiary engages in "non-homogeneous" operations.
consolidated, if the investor company has a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary or subsidiaries. this

1-19.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary that would require consolidation of the subsidiary’s financial statements with those of the parent?
o: Noncontrolling shareholders' substantive participating rights can prevent the majority shareholder from having a controlling financial interest.
Acontrolling financial interest is usually established by owning more than 50% of the outstanding voting shares of another entity.
Acontrolling financial interest can exist with less than a majority ownership percentage
Consolidation is required when a parent owns a majority voting interest in a subsidiary ven if a subsidiary operates under foreign restrictions that limit the parent’s control.
1-19.e: This statement is incorrect, so it is the correct answer to the question. If a majority-owned subsidiary operates under foreign restrictions, controls, or other governmentally imposed uncertainties that limit the parent's ability to control the subsidiary, the parent may not have a controlling financial interest. In that case, under the voting interest model the financial statements of the subsidiary should not be consolidated with those of the parent, even though the parent owns a majority voting interest in the subsidiary.
wrongWhy: This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest. If noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights, they can block or participate in significant financial and operating decisions, thereby preventing the majority shareholder from having a controlling financial interest.

This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary that would require consolidation. The usual condition for a controlling financial interest under the voting interest model is ownership by one reporting entity of more than % of the outstanding voting shares of another entity.

This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest. A controlling financial interest can exist with less than a majority ownership percentage. Ci PART SERTION A. aetna nancial gporin 220 2 er

1-19.q: Panco, Inc. owns 90% of the voting stock of Spany Corporation. After consolidated financial statements have been prepared, the entries to eliminate intercompany payables and receivables will
o: Not be reflected in the accounts of either company.
Be reflected in the accounts of both Panco and Spany.
Be reflected only in the accounts of Spany.
Be reflected only in the accounts of Panco.

Therefore, these intercompany payables and receivables cannot be eliminated from the accounts of the consolidated companies because they do still xist.

1-19.q: Alton Corporation purchased 100% of the shares of Jones Corporation for $600,000. Financial information for Jones Corporation is provided below. Cash Accounts receivable Inventory Total current assets Property, plant & equipment (net) Total assets Current liabilities Long-term debt Total liabilities Common stock Additional paid-in capital Retained earnings Total shareholders’ equity Total liabilities & shareholders’ equity Jones Corporation ___($000)_ Book Fair Value Value $ 50 $ 50 _150 _100 $300 $250 $800 $850 $150 $150 $350 $350 $150 $150 _220 $450 $800 The amount of goodwill or gain on a bargain purchase resulting from this purchase, if any, would be
o: Zero.
$100,000 goodwill.
$150,000 goodwill.
1-19.e: PART SERTION A. aetna nancial gporin 220 2 Ci v= 10:59 2024/8/15 CMA Part 1 — Test Bank — HOCK international Goodwill or gain on a bargain purchase is the difference between the total fair value of the acquired company (the fair value of the amount paid for the controlling interest plus the fair value of the noncontrolling interest, if any) and the fair value of the acquired company's identifiable net assets (total assets minus total liabilities). If the fair value of the acquired company is greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill. If the fair value of the acquired company is less than the fair value of the net assets acquired, the difference is a gain ona bargain purchase. The fair value of the total assets purchased is $850,000, and the fair value of the total liabilities assumed is $350,000. The difference, or $500,000, is the fair value of Jones Corporation's identifiable net assets. The difference between the fair value of the amount paid ($600,000) and the fair value of the identifiable net assets purchased ($500,000) is $100,000. Because the fair value of the amount paid for the acquired company was greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill.

If the fair value of the acquired company is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill. If the fair value of the acquired company is less than the fair value of the net assets acquired, the difference is a gain on a bargain purchase. Because the fair value of the amount paid for Jones Corporation was greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets acquired, goodwill needs to be recognized. If the fair value of the acquired company is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill. 1-19.q: In the process of preparing consolidated financial statements, which one of the following items does not need to be eliminated?
o: Gain on sale of a fixed asset to a subsidiary.
Profit on inventory sold to a nonaffiliate.
Profit in beginning inventory acquired from a parent.
Dividends receivable from a subsidiary. this
1-19.e: While the profit on inventory that is sold between affiliated companies that will be consolidated needs to be eliminated, the profit that is made on inventory sold to unaffiliated companies should not be eliminated in the consolidation process.
wrongWhy: Any gain on the sale of fixed assets sold to a subsidiary needs to be eliminated in the consolidation process.
The profit on inventory that is sold between companies that will be consolidated needs to be eliminated.
Dividends receivable from a subsidiary need to be eliminated in the consolidation process. PART SEATON A. etna nancial agporing 330 Q

1-19.q: In the context of consolidated financial statements, which of the following statements about noncontrolling interest is correct?
o: Noncontrolling interest is recorded as a liability on the consolidated balance sheet.
Noncontrolling interest is presented in the equity section of the consolidated balance sheet, representing the portion of a subsidiary not owned by the parent.
Noncontrolling interest does not impact the consolidated income statement since it only affects the balance sheet.
Noncontrolling interest represents the total value of all subsidiary companies owned by the parent company. this
1-19.e: In the consolidated balance sheet, the noncontrolling interest—the portion of the subsidiary that does not belong to the parent company—is shown as a separate caption in the stockholders’ equity section.
wrongWhy: Noncontrolling interest is not recorded as a liability on the consolidated balance sheet. Instead, it is included in the equity section of the consolidated balance sheet because it represents the equity interest in a subsidiary not attributable to the parent company.

Noncontrolling interest does affect the consolidated income statement. The net income attributable to the noncontrolling interest is separately identified and deducted from the consolidated net income to show the net income attributable to the parent company only.

Noncontrolling interest represents the portion of the subsidiary's net assets that are not owned by the parent company, not the total value of all subsidiary companies. The total value of all subsidiary companies is reflected in the consolidated financial statements. PART SERTION A. Femna Enansal Bgporing 230 Q 9g

1-19.q: Which of the following statements about Variable Interest Entities (VIEs) is correct?
o: AVIE is a legal entity that is financially controlled by a single company holding the majority voting interest.
Acompany can be considered the primary beneficiary of a VIE if it only has the obligation to absorb losses of the VIE that could be significant.
The definition of a parent in a consolidation excludes a VIE's primary beneficiary unless the beneficiary holds a majority voting interest.
The primary beneficiary of a VIE is required to consolidate the VIE's financial statements with its own regardless of the amount of ownership interest held. this
1-19.e: According to ASC 810-10, the financial statements of VIEs that have a primary beneficiary must be consolidated with the financial statements of the primary beneficiary regardless of the amount of ownership held by the primary beneficiary. This means that consolidation is based on the financial contro! and obligation to absorb losses or receive benefits from the VIE, not on the ownership interest percentage.
wrongWhy: This statement is incorrect because a VIE is typically controlled by one or more companies that do not hold a majority voting interest. The essence of a VIE is that control is achieved through other financial arrangements rather than voting rights.

This statement is incorrect because being the primary beneficiary of a VIE requires both the power to direct the activities that most impact the VIE’s economic performance and the obligation to absorb losses or receive benefits that could be significant. Both conditions must be met, not just one.

This statement is incorrect because the definition of a parent in a consolidation does include a VIE’s primary beneficiary, even if the beneficiary does not hold a majority voting interest. Control over the VIE is established through financial arrangements, not through voting rights. Ci /2

1-19.q: Financial statements of an investor and an investee should be consolidated when
o: acompany is financially controlled by another company that does not hold a majority voting interest in it.
noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights.
noncontrolling shareholders collectively hold more than 50% of the voting stock of an investee.
all of the affiliated companies are in the same industry. this
1-19.e: When a company or other legal entity is financially controlled by another company even though the controlling company does not hold a majority voting interest in it, the financial statements of the two companies must be consolidated under the variable interest entity (VIE) consolidation model.
wrongWhy: If noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights, they have the right to block or participate in significant financial and operating decisions made in the ordinary course of business, such as development of budgets. In that case, even if the investor company holds a majority of the outstanding voting shares, the investor company does not have a controlling financial interest and the financial statements should not be consolidated.

If noncontrolling shareholders collectively hold more than 50% of the voting stock of an investee, then the investor, with less than 50% ownership, probably does not have a controlling financial interest over the investee, and the investee’s financial statements should probably not be consolidated with the investor’s financial statements. The investment would probably be accounted for under the equity method.
Whether the affiliated companies are in the same industry or different industries is not relevant for determining whether their financial statements should be consolidated. Ci /2

1-20.q: Which cost is Capitalized as part of Pp&E?
o: Training employees to use the new machine
Installation costs to bring the machine to working condition
Administrative overhead allocated to the project
Marketing costs for new product made by the machine
1-20.e: Installation Costs that are necessary to bring the asset to its Intended Use are Capitalized as part of Pp&E cost. Training, admin overhead, and marketing are Period Costs expensed when incurred.

1-20.q: Land is Not depreciated because:
o: It is too expensive to track
Land has an Indefinite useful life — it does not wear out or become obsolete
GAAP does not allow it
It is always sold within one year
1-20.e: Land has an Indefinite useful life — it does not wear out, become obsolete, or be consumed. Only assets with Finite useful lives are depreciated. Land improvements (parking lots, fencing) Are depreciated because they have limited lives.

1-20.q: Interest incurred during the Self-Construction of an asset is:
o: Always expensed immediately
Capitalized — added to the cost of the asset during construction
Recorded as a deferred charge
Recorded in OCI
1-20.e: Capitalized Interest: when constructing an asset for own use, interest on related borrowings is Capitalized (added to asset cost) during the construction period. Once the asset is ready for use, interest is expensed as incurred.

1-20.q: Net Book Value (Nbv) of a fixed asset =
o: Historical Cost only
Historical Cost − Accumulated Depreciation
Fair Value at reporting date
Replacement Cost
1-20.e: Net Book Value (carrying value) = Historical Cost − Accumulated Depreciation. Nbv represents the unrecovered cost of the asset remaining on the balance sheet. It decreases each period as depreciation is recorded.

1-20.q: An Asset Retirement Obligation (Aro) relates to:
o: Annual maintenance costs
Estimated Future costs to dismantle/restore an asset — capitalized into Pp&E at inception
Tax deductions for depreciation
Financing costs for the asset
1-20.e: Aro: the estimated cost to Dismantle, Remove, and Restore a site is recognized as a Liability (Aro) and Capitalized into the Pp&E cost when the obligation arises. The asset (and obligation) are then recognized — the obligation is accreted over time.

1-20.q: Repair and maintenance costs on existing equipment are:
o: Capitalized as a betterment
Added to the cost of the asset
Expensed in the period incurred
Deferred and amortized over the asset's remaining life
1-20.e: Routine repair and maintenance costs (oil changes, cleaning, minor fixes) are Period Costs — expensed when incurred. Only Betterments (improvements that extend useful life, add capacity, or improve quality beyond original specifications) are capitalized.

1-20.q: The Cost of Pp&E includes all costs necessary to bring the asset to its intended location and condition. Which of the following is capitalized?
o: Insurance on the asset After it is placed in service
Cost of training employees to use the new machine
Freight and installation costs to set up the machine
Repair of existing equipment
1-20.e: Capitalize all costs to Acquire and Prepare the asset: purchase price, freight, installation, testing, and site preparation. Exclude: insurance after placed in service (operating expense), employee training (expense), repairs of existing assets.

1-20.q: A Lump-Sum (basket) purchase of land, building, and equipment for $900,000 when the appraised values are land $300,000, building $400,000, equipment $300,000 (total $1,000,000). The cost allocated to land is:
When Similar productive assets are exchanged and the exchange Lacks commercial substance:
o: Recognize the full gain on the exchange
Record the new asset at fair value of asset given up
Record no gain (defer it) — new asset = book value of old + cash paid
Recognize gain or loss based on book value difference
1-20.e: Exchange Without commercial substance: No Gain recognized (gain deferred — reduces basis of new asset). New asset = Nbv of old asset + cash paid (boot). Loss is always recognized immediately, even without commercial substance.

1-20.q: When Dissimilar assets (or exchanges With commercial substance) are exchanged:
o: No gain or loss is recognized
Record new asset at book value of old asset
Recognize full gain or loss — record new asset at fair value of asset given or received
Record at the lower of fair value or book value
1-20.e: Exchange With commercial substance: recognize Full Gain Or Loss. Record the new asset at Fair Value (of asset given up or received, whichever is more reliable). This reflects the economic substance of the exchange.

1-20.q: Under US GAAP, Government Grants received to acquire Pp&E are:
o: Recognized as revenue immediately
Deducted from the cost of the asset (reduces capitalized cost) Or deferred as unearned income and recognized over asset's life
Always treated as equity contributions
Expensed in the period received
1-20.e: US GAAP (ASC 958 for NFPs; general guidance for for-profit entities) generally allows either: reduce the Asset'S Cost by the grant, or record as Deferred Income amortized over the asset's life. Under IFRS (Ias 20), the same two treatments are explicitly permitted.

1-20.q: A Betterment (capital improvement) to an existing asset is:
o: Expensed immediately as a repair
Capitalized — it extends useful life or improves functionality beyond original specifications
Disclosed in notes only
Amortized separately over 5 years regardless of asset life
1-20.e: Betterments (improvements, additions, replacements that Enhance the asset beyond its original capability or extend its useful life) are Capitalized. Ordinary repairs and maintenance that merely Maintain the asset = Expensed.

1-20.q: Under IFRS, Component Depreciation requires:
o: Depreciating the entire asset as a single unit
Identifying significant components with different useful lives and depreciating each separately
Applying a single depreciation rate to all assets
Revaluing components annually to fair value
1-20.e: IFRS (Ias 16) Requires component depreciation for assets with significant components having Different useful lives (e.g., aircraft engines vs. fuselage). US GAAP permits but does not require it. Each component has its own depreciation schedule.

1-20.q: Under the Revaluation Model (IFRS only), when Pp&E is revalued Upward above original cost:
o: The increase is recognized in net income
The increase goes to Other Comprehensive Income (Revaluation Surplus in equity)
The asset is written up and a gain recognized immediately
Depreciation stops after revaluation
1-20.e: Under IFRS revaluation model: Upward revaluations above original cost → credit to Revaluation Surplus in Other Comprehensive Income (equity). Only revaluation Reversals of previous downward revaluations can go to income. US GAAP does Not allow upward revaluation.

1-20.q: For a Self-Constructed asset, overhead is:
o: Never allocated — only direct costs are capitalized
Allocated to the asset using a reasonable method (e.g., proportionate share of actual overhead)
Expensed as incurred regardless of capitalization
Added only if the overhead is directly traceable to construction
1-20.e: Self-constructed assets are capitalized at the Full Cost of construction: direct materials, direct labor, And a Reasonable Allocation of overhead. Both incremental overhead and a share of fixed overhead may be allocated. The amount capitalized should not exceed fair value.

1-20.q: Pp&E acquired by issuing the company's Own Stock is recorded at:
o: Par value of the stock issued
Book value per share × shares issued
Fair value of the stock issued (or fair value of the asset received, whichever is more reliably measurable)
The most conservative (lower) of the two fair values
1-20.e: Non-cash asset acquisitions are recorded at Fair Value — whichever is more reliably measurable: fair value of the Stock Issued or fair value of the Asset Received. Par value is irrelevant for this measurement.

1-20.q: Land Improvements (e.g., parking lots, fencing, landscaping) differ from Land because:
o: Land improvements are not capitalized — they are expensed
Land improvements are Depreciable — they have limited lives; land itself has an indefinite life
Land improvements are classified as intangible assets
Land and land improvements are depreciated together
1-20.e: Land has an Indefinite useful life — never depreciated. Land Improvements (parking lots, fences, drainage, paving) have Finite lives and Are depreciated. This is a classic exam distinction.

1-20.q: Internal-use Computer Software development costs under US GAAP are:
o: Always expensed as R&D
Capitalized during the Application Development stage; preliminary project stage and post-implementation stage are expensed
Capitalized once technological feasibility is established
Always capitalized regardless of development stage
1-20.e: Internal-use software (ASC 350-40): Three stages: (1) Preliminary project — Expense; (2) Application development — Capitalize; (3) Post-implementation/operation — Expense. Technological feasibility is the IFRS/software-for-sale standard, not internal-use.

1-20.q: Website Development costs under US GAAP follow a similar model to internal-use software. Costs during the Planning stage are:
o: Capitalized as an intangible asset
Expensed as incurred
Deferred until the website launches
Amortized over 3 years starting from incurrence
1-20.e: Website development (Eitf 00-2, now ASC 350): Planning stage = Expense. Application/infrastructure development stage = Capitalize. Graphics development = capitalize. Content population = generally expense (unless content itself gives future benefit). Post-launch maintenance = expense.

1-20.q: An Asset Retirement Obligation (Aro) is initially measured at:
o: The estimated undiscounted future cost of retirement
The Present Value of the estimated future retirement costs, using a credit-adjusted risk-free rate
Zero — until the asset is retired
The replacement cost of the asset
1-20.e: An Aro is initially measured at its Fair Value — typically the Present Value of estimated future retirement costs, discounted at a credit-adjusted risk-free rate. An offsetting Asset Retirement Cost is capitalized and added to the carrying value of the related asset.

1-21.q: Nella Corporation computes depreciation to the nearest whole month. A new piece of quipment was placed in operation on July 1, 20X1. 1-21.q: Nella Corporation computes depreciation to the nearest whole month. A new piece of equipment was placed in operation on July 1, 20X71. It was expected to produce 400,000 units of product in its estimated useful life of eight years. Total cost was $300,000; salvage value was estimated to be $30,000. Nella employs a calendar year for financial reporting purposes. Actual production for the past 3 years was as follows. Year 1 - 34,000 units Year 2 - 62,500 units Year 3 - 58,400 units If Nella uses the sum-of-the-years'-digits method of depreciation, the amount of depreciation computed for this quipment for book purposes in 20X3 would be
1-21.e: Under the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, the depreciation expense is calculated as the depreciable amount (original cost - salvage value) multiplied by a fraction. For the amount of annual depreciation, the numerator of the fraction is the number of years remaining in the asset's useful life, and the denominator is the sum of all of its xpected years of life. For an asset with an 8-year life, the denominatoris8+7+6+5+4+3+2+1=36. Since Nella calculates depreciation to the nearest whole month and this asset was purchased on July 1, the amount of depreciation in the first year (20X1) will be only half of a year's depreciation. Therefore, in 20X1 the fraction will be 4/36. This is the same as taking half of the first year's depreciation in 20X1, or 0.5 x 8/36. In 20X2 it will be 7.5/36. This is the same as taking half of the first year's depreciation (0.5 x 8/36) and half of the second year's depreciation (0.5 x 7/36). In 20X3 the fraction to use will be 6.5/36. This fraction is multiplied by the depreciable amount, which is the cost minus the salvage value. The cost of the asset is $300,000 and the salvage value is $30,000, giving a depreciable amount of $270,000. Multiplying $270,000 by 6.5/36 we get the 20X3 depreciation expense of $48,750.
wrongWhy: This is the depreciation for the second full year of the asset's life, or the amount that would be recorded from July X2 through June 30, 20X3. It is not the amount that would be recorded from January 1, 20X3 through December 31, 20X3. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This is the depreciation for the third full year of the asset's life, or the amount that would be recorded from July 1, X3 through June 30, 20X4. It is not the amount that would be recorded from January 1, 20X3 through December X3. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This is equal to one-half of the depreciation for the fourth full year of the asset's life, or the amount that would be recorded from July 1, 20X4 through December 31, 20X4. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. PART SERTION A. aetna nancial Bgporing 330 Q 83 /2

1-21.q: Pearl Corporation acquired manufacturing machinery on January 1 for $9,000. During the year, the machine produced 1,000 units, of which 600 were sold. There was no work-in- process inventory at the beginning or at the end of the year. Installation charges of $300 and delivery charges of $200 were also incurred. The machine is expected to have a useful life of five years with an estimated salvage value of $1,500. Pearl uses the straight-line depreciation method. The original cost of the machinery to be recorded in Pear!'s books is
o: $9,000 this
1-21.e: Fixed assets are originally recorded at all of the costs necessary to get the asset ready for use. This includes the cost of the asset itself, shipping, installation, testing, insurance during transit, any import taxes, and any other costs that needed to be incurred in order to prepare the asset for its intended use. Therefore, Pearl should include the $9,000 cost of the asset as well as the installation charges of $300 and the delivery charges of $200, for a total of $9,500. This $9,500 minus the estimated salvage value of $1,500 will be used to calculate the straight-line depreciation over the life of the asset.
wrongWhy: The installation charges of $300 should also be included in the initial recorded cost of the asset.
The delivery charges of $200 should also be included in the initial recorded cost of the asset.
The delivery charges and the installation charges should be included in the initial recorded cost of the asset. Ci

1-21.q: Baby Products Ltd. does its financial reporting under IFRS. You have recently been hired as a Certified Management Accountant for the firm. The CFO asks you to assist in determining the correct amount of Other Comprehensive Income based on the following line items: Foreign currency translation loss €25,000 Unrealized gain on available-for-sale debt securities 32,000 Foreign currency remeasurement gain 6,000 Revaluation increase on fixed assets 12,000 Unrealized loss on trading debt securities 4,000 Revaluation loss on intangible assets 15,000 Deferred pension gain 23,000 Deferred gain on a cash flow hedge 11,000
1-21.e: Comprehensive Income includes everything on the Profit or Loss Statement plus some specific items that do not appear on the Profit or Loss Statement that are called “Other Comprehensive Income” and are recognized in Equity. Under IFRS, an item of property, plant and equipment whose fair value can be measured reliably is carried at a revalued amount equal to its fair value at the date of the revaluation less any subsequent accumulated depreciation and subsequent accumulated impairment losses. Revaluation increases are recognized as Other Comprehensive Income unless they reverse a previous revaluation decrease. (Revaluation decreases are recognized in Profit or Loss unless they reverse a previous revaluation increase.) Foreign currency translation gains and losses are reported in Other Comprehensive Income, whereas foreign currency remeasurement gains and losses are reported in Profit or Loss.

Under IFRS, revaluation increases of fixed assets are permitted (unlike under US GAAP, which does not permit revaluation increases of fixed assets). Under IFRS, revaluation increases are reported as Other Comprehensive Income.

This is the foreign currency translation loss, the unrealized gain on AFS debt securities, the revaluation increase on fixed assets, the revaluation loss on intangible assets, the deferred pension gain, and the deferred gain on a cash flow hedge. In this question the lathe has an eight year useful life. With an eight year useful life the depreciation under the straight-line method would be 12.5% per year. Therefore, in order to calculate the depreciation in the second year, we will also need to calculate the depreciation expense for the first year for the lathe. Remember also that the cost of the asset includes all of the costs necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use. In the first year the depreciation expense was $10,800 ($43,200 x 0.25), which reduced the carrying value to $32,400 for year 2. Maple uses the mid year convention meaning that only six months of depreciation is taken in the first year that the asset is owned. This is a little bit confusing in this question since the asset was purchased on June 1, but what this means is that for the period from June 1, year 1 - May 31, year they will take only 6 months of depreciation. Under the straight line method we simply divide the depreciable amount (cost - salvage value) by the estimated useful life to get the annual depreciation. In this question, we will need to divide this by 2 to get the first year's depreciation. The depreciable amount is $39,600 ($43,200 - $3,600) and the useful life is 8 years. 1-21.q: Majesty Amusement Park recently installed a new thrill ride. Although this attraction has an average life of 40 years, Majesty estimates that the ride will be popular for 15 years, at which point it will be disassembled and replaced with a different ride. Park attendance is based upon the local economy, which is difficult to predict. The method Majesty should use to depreciate this ride is
o: declining balance.
straight-line with a 15-year life.
units-of-output.
straight-line with a 40-year life.
1-21.e: Amusement park ride usage is dependent on park attendance. The question says, "park attendance is based upon the local economy, which is difficult to predict." That is the only fact given that would support any assumption as to ride usage. Therefore, the ride should be depreciated over 15 years because that is its estimated useful life, and the straight-line method should be used. Majesty has no reason to believe that the ride will be used more heavily at the beginning of its useful life than toward the end of its useful life, and thus an accelerated method such as the declining balance method should not be used. The company also has no way of predicting the total number of rides that will taken during the useful life of the ride, so the units-of-output method of depreciation cannot be used.
wrongWhy: Majesty has no reason to believe that the ride will be used more heavily at the beginning of its useful life than toward the end of its useful life, so an accelerated method of depreciation such as a declining balance method should not be used. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Majesty has no way of predicting the total number of rides that will taken during the useful life of the ride, so the company would not be able to develop a depreciation cost per unit. Therefore, the units- of-output method of depreciation cannot be used. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The ride's estimated useful life is 15 years, so it should not be depreciated over a 40-year life. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. fe PART SERTION A. aetna nancial gporin 220 2 8 ay

1-21.q: Aston Company acquired a new machine at a cost of $200,000 and incurred costs of $2,000 to have the machine shipped to its factory. Aston also paid $4,500 to construct and prepare a site for the new machine and $3,500 to install the necessary electrical connections. Aston estimates that the useful life of this new machine will be 5 years and that it will have a salvage value of $15,000 at the end of that period. Assuming that Aston acquired the machine on January 1 and will take a full year's depreciation in the first year of ownership, the proper amount of depreciation expense to be recorded by Aston for the first year if it uses the double-declining-balance method is
1-21.e: The first step is to include the initial cost of the asset. Fixed assets are originally recorded at all of the costs necessary to get the asset ready for use. This includes the cost of the asset itself, shipping, installation, testing, insurance during transit, any import taxes, and any other costs that needed to be incurred in order to prepare the asset for its intended use. Therefore, Aston should include the $200,000 cost, the $2,00 in shipping, the $4,500 in construction of a new site and $3,500 in electrical connections. This totals $210,000. Under the double declining balance method depreciation is calculated as the book value of the asset multiplied by twice what the straight-line percentage would be. The asset has a useful life of 5 years so under the straight-line method depreciation would be 20% per year. Twice this is 40% so Aston should take % of the book value of the asset as depreciation expense. The book value was $210,000 for the first year and 40% of this is $84,000. Note that under the double declining balance method the salvage value of the asset is not needed until later in the life of the asset. Salvage value is used only at the end to make sure that the asset is not depreciated below the salvage value.
wrongWhy: This answer incorrectly includes the salvage value in the calculation of depreciation and also does not include the costs of acquisition and installation that should be included in the asset cost. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer does not include all of the costs of acquisition in the calculation of the asset cost. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer incorrectly takes into account the salvage value when calculating the depreciation expense. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. /2

1-21.q: When a fixed plant asset with a 5-year estimated useful life is sold during the second year, how would the use of an accelerated depreciation method instead of the straight-line method affect the gain or loss on the sale of the fixed plant asset?
o: Again would decrease, and a loss would decrease.
Again would increase, and a loss would increase.
Again would increase, and a loss would decrease.
Again would decrease, and a loss would increase. this
1-21.e: The accelerated method will depreciate the asset faster in the early years of the asset's life than the straight-line method would, thereby reducing its book value faster. As a result, any gain on a sale during the second year would be greater with the accelerated method than with the straight-line method and any loss would be less with the accelerated method than with the straight-line method.
wrongWhy: The accelerated method would cause the gain to be greater than with straight-line depreciation, not less.
The use of an accelerated method would decrease a loss since assets are depreciated at a faster rate in the early years under an accelerated method than they are under the straight- line method.

The accelerated method will depreciate the asset faster than the straight-line method would in the early years, thereby reducing its book value faster than would be the case under the straight-line method. Ci

1-21.q: Anewly acquired plant asset is to be depreciated over its useful life. The best rationale for this process is the
o: Matching principle.
Monetary unit assumption.
Materiality requirement.
Revenue recognition principle..
1-21.e: According to the FASB's Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8 (2021), Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, expenses are outflows or other using up of assets of an entity or incurrences of its liabilities (or a combination of both) from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or carrying out other activities. Therefore, the matching principle states that expenses should be recognized not when payment is made but when the work for which the payment is made contributes to revenue. Depreciation is the systematic and rational allocation of the cost of an asset when conomic benefits are expected to arise over several accounting periods and the association with income can be only indirectly determined. It is a process of trying to match the revenues that the asset generates with the expense of the asset.
wrongWhy: The monetary unit assumption is not the rationale for depreciating an asset over its useful life. Money serves as the standard unit of measurement to measure economic activity and the transactions a company undertakes. For a given country, the currency in use in that country is the monetary unit assumption. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

1-21.q: International Industries, Inc. purchased a laser additive manufacturing (LAM) 3-D production machine for £900,000. The machine was expected to have a life of 10 years, but the expected life of the laser component of the equipment was only 5 years. Both the laser and the main part of the LAM machine are being depreciated using straight line depreciation. At the end of 5 years, International Industries replaced the laser at a cost of £250,000. What is the future annual depreciation charge on the LAM machine, including the laser, after the replacement of the laser?
1-21.e: Under IFRS, if individual components of a large fixed asset have different usage patterns and/or different useful lives, the individual components are depreciated separately. The laser has a shorter useful life than the main machine has. Therefore, the laser is depreciated separately from the main machine. The annual depreciation charge on the main machine will be the same as it has been: (£680,000 - £20,000) + 10 = £66,000. Since no residual value was assigned to the new laser for the purpose of calculating its depreciable base, the annual depreciation charge on the newly-installed laser component of the machine will be its full cost divided by the number of years of estimated useful life: £250,000 + 5 = £50,000. 1-21.q: International Industries, Inc. purchased a laser additive manufacturing (LAM) 3-D production machine for £900,000. The machine was expected to have a life of 10 years, but the expected life of the laser component of the equipment was only 5 years. The cost allocated to the laser component was £220,000, with a residual value of £10,000. The cost allocated to the main part of the machine was £680,000 with a residual value of £20,000. Both the laser and the main part of the LAM machine are being depreciated using straight line depreciation. At the end of 5 years, International Industries replaced the laser at a cost of £250,000. No residual value was assigned to the replacement laser. The original laser was sold for £2,000. International Industries uses IFRS for its financial reporting. What was the carrying value of the LAM machine, including the laser, just before the replacement of the laser?
1-21.e: Under IFRS, if individual components of a large fixed asset have different usage patterns and/or different useful lives, the individual components are depreciated separately. The laser has a shorter useful life than the main machine has. Therefore, the laser is depreciated separately from the main machine. The annual depreciation charge for the main machine is (£680,000 — £20,000) + 10 = £66,000. The annual depreciation charge for the laser component is (£220,000 - £10,000) + 5 = £42,000. Thus the total annual depreciation charge (for the first five years until the laser is fully depreciated) is £66,000 + £42,000 = £108,000. Therefore, the depreciation booked over the first five years is £108,000 x 5 = £540,000. Which of the following depreciation methods will result in the greatest amount of depreciation expense for this machine in its first year?
o: Sum of the years' digits.
Double declining balance method.
Straight line.
Activity method based on units of production..

1-21.e: Under the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, the depreciation expense is calculated as the depreciable amount (original cost - salvage value) multiplied by a fraction. This fraction is multiplied by the depreciable amount, which is the cost minus the salvage value. The cost of the car is $4,800 and the salvage value was $600, giving a depreciable amount of $4,200. 1-21.q: Blake Ltd. has determined that an impairment exists on one of its machines, but the company xpects to continue using the asset for another three full years as no active market exists for the machine. Selected information on the impaired asset (on the date that impairment was determined to exist) is provided below. Original cost of machine £22,000 Book (carrying) value of the machine 20,000 Value in use (present value of future cash flows) 15,000 Net selling price (fair value if sold less costs to sell) 12,000 According to IFRS, what is the amount of the impairment loss to be recorded by Blake?
1-21.e: Under IFRS, the amount of an impairment loss is the difference between the carrying amount of the asset and the recoverable amount. The recoverable amount is the higher of (1) the fair value of the asset if sold minus the cost to sell, and (2) the asset's value in use, which is the present value of the future cash flows to be generated by the asset in use, including its residual value. The carrying value of the machine is £20,000. The machine's value in use (the present value of the future cash flows) is £15,000. The net selling price, or the fair value if sold less costs to sell, is £12,000. The recoverable amount is the greater of £15,000 or £12,000, so the recoverable amount is £15,000. The impairment loss—the difference between the carrying value of the £20,000 and the recoverable amount of £15,000—is £5,000.
