1-22.q: How does IFRS differ from U.S. GAAP with respect to accounting for development costs?
o: U.S. GAAP treats development costs as part of goodwill, whereas IFRS treats these costs as an intangible asset.
U.S. GAAP requires capitalization of development costs, whereas IFRS makes capitalization of these costs optional.
U.S. GAAP requires expensing of all development costs and IFRS requires capitalizing all development costs.

U.S. GAAP does not allow capitalization of development costs unless they are for materials, equipment, or facilities that have alternative future uses. IFRS allows capitalization of development costs but only if technical feasibility has been stablished.
1-22.e: Under U.S. GAAP, research and development costs are expensed as incurred, per ASC 730- -25-1. Development costs may not be capitalized unless they are for materials, equipment, or facilities that have alternative future uses, for R&D or otherwise, according to ASC 730-10-25-2. IFRS does permit capitalization of development costs, but only if technical feasibility has been stablished. IAS 38.57, specifies that development costs may be recognized as an intangible asset if and only if an entity can demonstrate all of the following: (a) technical feasibility of completing the asset so it will be available for use or sale; (b) its intention to complete the asset and use or sell it; (c) its ability to use or sell the intangible asset; (d) how the intangible asset will generate probable future economic benefits; (e) the availability of adequate technical, financial and other resources to complete the development and to use or sell the intangible asset; (f) its ability to measure reliably the expenditure attributable to the intangible asset during its development.
wrongWhy: U.S. GAAP does not treat development costs as part of goodwill, and IFRS does not treat these costs as an intangible asset except under very specific circumstances.
U.S. GAAP does not require capitalization of development costs, and IFRS does not make capitalization of these costs optional.
IFRS does not require capitalizing all development costs. Ci /2

1-22.q: Pie Baker, Ltd. purchased a secret fruit pie recipe for $75,000. An additional $10,000 was spent in securing the secret recipe and safeguarding its contents. Pie Baker expects to keep the recipe a secret indefinitely. Because of taste changes, the industry has found that recipes have been used for an average of 8 years. Based on this information, Pie Baker should
o: Capitalize the $85,000 cost and then amortize it over the period the recipe is to remain a secret.
Capitalize the $85,000 cost and amortize it over 8 years.
Expense the $85,000 cost because the secret formula cost should not be capitalized.
Capitalize the $85,000 cost and then amortize it over 40 years.
1-22.e: This is an example of a process patent, since it is a trade secret. If a patent is purchased from an inventor or other owner, the purchase price represents its cost and the cost is to be capitalized. Other costs incurred in connection with securing a patent, attorney's fees and other unrecovered costs of a successful legal suit to protect the patent, can be capitalized as part of the patent cost. However, research and development costs related to the development of the product, process, or idea that is subsequently patented must be expensed as incurred. The cost of a patent should be amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. In this case, the expected useful life of the recipe is 8 years. The fact that the recipe will be kept a secret indefinitely is irrelevant. Therefore, the cost of the recipe, $85,000, should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years.
wrongWhy: This is an example of a process patent. If a patent is purchased from its original developer, the cost of the patent should be capitalized and amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. Thus, the purchase price should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years. The length of time the recipe is to remain a secret is irrelevant.

This is an example of a process patent. If a patent is purchased from its original developer, the cost of the patent should be capitalized and amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. Thus, the purchase price should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years.

The maximum amortization period for a patent (and a secret recipe is a process patent) is 20 years, and that would apply only if the patent were owned from the date it was granted and was expected to be useful for its maximum legal life of 20 years. In this case the patent (i.e., recipe) has been purchased from the original developer. Furthermore, it is expected to have a useful life of only 8 years. The cost of the patent should be amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. In this case, that is 8 years. Ci /2

1-22.q: An employee of M, a public company, developed a new product that has just been patented. The development costs of this product were negligible, but the patent rights are almost certainly worth many millions of dollars. Which accounting concept would prevent the company from recognizing the value of this patent as a non-current asset in its balance sheet?
o: Going concern
Materiality
Historical cost
Conservatism
1-22.e: Because this was an internally generated asset, there is no transaction with which to measure the true value of the patent. In this case, the development costs were almost zero. But even if the development costs had been substantial, they would not have been capitalized as an asset on the balance sheet. A patent is an intangible asset, and the only costs of patents that can be capitalized as assets on the balance sheet are costs incurred in connection with securing the patent such as registration fees and attorney's fees for filing the patent application. Research and development costs related to the development of a product, process, or idea that is subsequently patented must be xpensed as incurred.
wrongWhy: The going concern concept says that a company's balance sheet must reflect the value of that company assuming it will remain in existence for, and beyond, the foreseeable future. The going concern concept would not prevent the company from recognizing the value of the patent as a non- current asset in its balance sheet.

Materiality is a constraint that accounting is subject to. An item of information is material and should be reported if it is judged to be significant enough that it would have an effect on a decision maker. Whether or not an item of information is material depends upon the relative size of the item, the precision with which it can be estimated, and the nature of the item. Materiality would not prevent the company from recognizing the value of the patent as a non-current asset in its balance sheet.

The conservatism concept means that when in doubt, choose the solution that will be least likely to overstate assets and income. However, there would be no cause for doubt in the decision of whether or not to recognize the value of the patent as a non-current asset in the company's balance sheet. Ci /2

1-22.q: Which one of the following statements is correct about the reconciliation of U.S. GAAP and International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)?
o: Internally generated goodwill may not be capitalized under U.S. GAAP, but it may be capitalized under IFRS.
The costs of research must be expensed under U.S. GAAP but are capitalized under IFRS if they meet specific criteria.
AIl costs of research and development must be expensed under both U.S. GAAP and IFRS.
The costs of development must be expensed under U.S. GAAP but are capitalized under IFRS if they meet specific criteria.
1-22.e: Costs of internal development must be expensed as incurred under U.S. GAAP. Under IFRS, internal development expenditures are capitalized if technical and economic feasibility of a project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria, including intent to complete the asset and ability to sell the asset in the future.
wrongWhy: The cost of internally-generated intangible assets are not recorded on the balance sheet under ither standard.

This statement is not true. Both U.S. GAAP and IFRS require research costs to be expensed as incurred. However, note that development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. Under U.S. GAAP, development costs are generally expensed as incurred. Under IFRS, development costs are capitalized when the technological and economic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated.

Internal costs related to the research phase of research and development are expensed as incurred under both standards. However, development costs are accounted for differently under the two standards. https:thwmw. ton A Pe Sana, Fdemal Financial Reporting 330Q 148 1/2

1-22.q: All of the following are specifically identifiable intangible assets except
o: Leaseholds.
Copyrights.
Patents and trademarks.
Goodwill.
1-22.e: Goodwill is not a specifically identifiable intangible asset. Rather, it is an unidentifiable intangible asset because it is not known specifically what the asset arises from.
wrongWhy: Leasehold improvements are specifically identifiable intangible assets.
Acopyright is a specifically identifiable intangible asset.
Patents and trademarks are specifically identifiable intangible assets. Ci

1-22.q: According to ASC 730-10-25-2, Research and Development-Elements of Costs to Be Identified with Research and Development Activities, expenditures for equipment for research and development
o: must be expensed in the period incurred, unless the equipment has alternative uses in other R&D projects or otherwise.
may be expensed in the period incurred or capitalized if the probability of future benefits can readily be determined.
must be capitalized in the period incurred and amortized over the estimated life of the asset.
must be expensed in the period incurred, unless the costs are for testing a prototype.
1-22.e: Research and development costs are usually expensed as they are incurred. However, if the quipment will have an alternative use on other R&D projects or otherwise, the asset may be capitalized and depreciated. When it is used in R&D projects, the depreciation expense should be recorded as R&D expense and not as depreciation expense.
wrongWhy: R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise. The determination of future benefits is not the criterion used to determine if the asset may be depreciated.

R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise.

R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise. Whether the costs are for testing a prototype is not the criterion used for determining whether the asset may be capitalized. Ci

1-22.q: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the accounting treatment of research and development costs (R&D) under U.S. GAAP and IFRS?
o: U.S. GAAP requires R&D to be expensed, and IFRS requires research costs to be xpensed but allows for the capitalization of the development costs.
Neither U.S. GAAP nor IFRS allow for costs of R&D to be capitalized.
U.S. GAAP allows for the capitalization of the costs of R&D, and IFRS requires R&D costs to be expensed.
Both U.S. GAAP and IFRS allow for costs of R&D to be capitalized.
1-22.e: The accounting treatment for research costs is similar in U.S. GAAP and IFRS: both sets of standards require research expenses to be expensed. However, development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. Under U.S. GAAP, internal development costs are usually expensed as incurred. They may be capitalized if and only if a specific U.S. GAAP standard allows capitalization for that asset. Under IFRS, internal development costs may be capitalized under certain circumstances when the technological and conomic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated.
wrongWhy: It is true that neither U.S. GAAP nor IFRS allows for research costs to be capitalized. However development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. U.S. GAAP requires development costs to be expensed as incurred, but IFRS does allow for development costs to be capitalized under certain circumstances. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

U.S. GAAP does not allow for the capitalization of the costs of either research or development. IFRS requires research costs to be expensed, but under IFRS, development costs may be capitalized if technical and economic feasibility of a project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

U.S. GAAP does not allow for costs of either research or development to be capitalized. IFRS does not allow research costs to be capitalized, although development costs may be capitalized under IFRS when the technological and economic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Ci /2

1-23.q: Long-term debt is reclassified as current liability when:
o: Management decides to pay it early
It matures within One Year of the Balance Sheet date
Interest payments are past due
The credit rating declines
1-23.e: Long-term debt becomes a Current liability when it is Due Within One Year of the Balance Sheet date. The principal amount maturing within 12 months must be shown as current to properly reflect the company's near-term cash obligations.

1-23.q: A debt covenant is violated before year-end. The lender has not waived the acceleration clause. This debt should be classified as:
o: Non-current — covenant violations are normal
Current — the lender could demand immediate repayment
Only disclosed in notes, not reclassified
Current only if the lender actually demands payment
1-23.e: When a Covenant is violated and the lender can demand immediate repayment, the debt should be classified as Current. The acceleration risk means the debt could require cash within 12 months. Classification follows economic substance.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, if long-term debt matures in 6 months but the company completes a refinancing agreement (3-year term) One Month After the BS date:
o: Can be classified as long-term because refinancing occurred
Must remain current — refinancing was Not completed before BS date
Can be long-term if disclosed in notes
Is classified based on management intent
1-23.e: Under GAAP: refinancing must be Completed (not just agreed upon) Before the BS date to classify debt as long-term. If refinancing occurs after the BS date — even before FS issuance — the debt remains Current under GAAP (IFRS differs here).

1-23.q: A lender grants a covenant waiver for 18 months from the Balance Sheet date. The violated long-term debt should be classified as:
o: Current — violations are always current
Non-Current — waiver covers more than 12 months
Current — waivers don't change classification
Non-current only if management intends to refinance
1-23.e: If the lender grants a Waiver covering at least 12 months from the BS date (here 18 months > 12 months), the acceleration risk is eliminated for that period. The debt can remain Non-Current because the lender has given up the right to demand payment for the waiver period.

1-23.q: Under IFRS, if a company refinances violating long-term debt Before the FS are authorized for issue (but after BS date), the debt:
o: Must always be current
May be classified as non-current since refinancing completed before FS authorized
Is always non-current
Must be disclosed but not reclassified
1-23.e: IFRS allows the refinancing to be considered if completed before the FS Are Authorized For Issue (even after BS date). This is a Key difference from GAAP, which only considers refinancing completed Before the BS date itself.

1-23.q: A Demand Note (callable by lender at any time) is classified as:
o: Long-term
Current — lender can demand payment within the year
Long-term if company does not expect demand
Non-current if secured by long-term assets
1-23.e: Demand notes callable At Lender'S Discretion = Current liabilities. The lender could legally demand full repayment anytime. Intent does not override the contractual right.

1-23.q: Reclassifying $500,000 of long-term debt to current due to covenant violation affects working capital by:
o: Decreasing working capital by $500,000
Increasing working capital
No effect
Decreasing current assets by $500,000
1-23.e: Working capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Moving $500,000 to Current Liabilities increases current liabilities → working capital Decreases by $500,000.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, short-term debt Can be classified as long-term if, before the FS are Issued, the company:
o: Signs a letter of intent
Obtains a Firm refinancing commitment (or actually refinances long-term)
Has board approval
Discloses intent in notes
1-23.e: ASC 470-10: must Complete refinancing or have Firm Commitment before FS are Issued (not just authorized). Intent alone is insufficient.

1-23.q: The Key difference between IFRS and US GAAP on covenant-violated debt is:
o: They are identical
Under IFRS, waiver before FS are Authorized For Issue (not just issued) keeps debt long-term
Under GAAP, waiver must be before FS authorization
IFRS always requires current classification
1-23.e: IFRS: waiver before FS Authorized For Issue = long-term. US GAAP: waiver before FS Issued. These dates can differ — IFRS allows a slightly longer window.

1-23.q: Current Portion Of Long-Term Debt =
o: 12 months of scheduled payments
Principal due within one year from BS date (or operating cycle if longer)
Callable portion
Annual interest payment
1-23.e: Current portion = debt principal due within One Year from the balance sheet date. Reclassified from long-term to current regardless of original issuance date.

1-23.q: A Subjective Acceleration Clause triggers when:
o: Borrower misses a payment
There is a Material Adverse Change in borrower financial condition
Lender needs liquidity
Market rates change
1-23.e: Subjective acceleration clauses use vague 'material adverse change' triggers. If acceleration probability is remote → debt may remain long-term. If reasonably possible or probable → reclassify to current.

1-23.q: For a waiver to keep covenant-violated debt as long-term, the waiver must cover:
o: At least 30 days
At least 6 months
More Than 12 Months from the balance sheet date
Indefinitely
1-23.e: Waiver must defer acceleration for More Than 12 Months from the BS date to maintain long-term classification.

1-23.q: A company violates a covenant September 30 (year-end). Lender grants waiver October 15 (before FS issued). Under US GAAP:
o: Current — violation existed at BS date
Long-term — waiver obtained before FS issued
Long-term only if waiver was before year-end
Current — waivers must be before year-end
1-23.e: US GAAP: waiver (for 12+ months) obtained before FS Issued = long-term, even if after the balance sheet date.

1-23.q: A $1,000,000 note maturing in 3 years has $200,000 due in next 12 months. Balance sheet shows:
o: $1,000,000 current
$200,000 current, $800,000 long-term
$1,000,000 long-term
All current — single note
1-23.e: $200,000 = Current Portion of long-term debt (due within 12 months). $800,000 = Long-Term. Split even for a single note.

1-23.q: Bridge Financing is classified as:
o: Long-term immediately — intended to be refinanced
Current unless a firm long-term commitment exists before FS issuance
Long-term with board approval
Current only if expiring within 30 days
1-23.e: Bridge financing = current until a Firm Commitment for permanent long-term financing exists (before FS issuance). Intent alone → current.

1-23.q: Minimum disclosure when short-term debt is excluded from current liabilities includes:
o: No disclosure needed
Description, terms of new arrangement, and amount excluded
Amount only
Auditor confirmation
1-23.e: Must disclose: (1) Description of refinancing, (2) Terms of new arrangement, (3) Amount excluded from current liabilities.

1-23.q: Under IFRS (Ias 1), covenant-violated long-term debt is classified as current Unless:
o: Company intends to cure
Lender grants waiver — at any time
Waiver granted Before FS authorized for issue (for 12+ months)
Violation is technical/immaterial
1-23.e: IFRS: must obtain waiver Before FS Authorized For Issue (for 12+ months post-BS). Under GAAP, cutoff is before FS Issued — potentially a later date.

1-23.q: Long-term debt due On Demand must be classified as:
o: Long-term if management expects to refinance
Current unless creditor has Written Waiver of demand right for 12+ months
Long-term if secured by fixed assets
Current only if demand has been made
1-23.e: Due-on-demand debt = Current unless written Waiver confirms demand right won't be exercised for more than 12 months from BS date.

1-23.q: A Revolving Credit Facility expiring in 14 months is classified as:
o: Current — expires within normal operating cycle
Long-Term — committed facility extends beyond 12 months
Current or long-term by management intent
Long-term only if currently drawn
1-23.e: Revolving credit with expiration beyond 12 months from BS date = Long-Term. The committed availability is long-term regardless of current borrowings.

1-23.q: Company intends to refinance short-term note but has No financing commitment. Classification:
o: Long-term — stated intent
Current — intent alone insufficient under GAAP
Long-term if disclosed in footnotes
Long-term if probable
1-23.e: Intent Alone is Insufficient. A Firm Commitment must exist before FS are issued. Without it — Current.

1-24.q: An Assurance-Type warranty is accounted for by:
o: Deferring all warranty revenue until claims are paid
Accruing a warranty expense and liability at the time of sale
Recording warranty costs only when claims are made
Treating it as a separate performance obligation
1-24.e: Assurance warranty: accrue Warranty Expense and Warranty Liability at the time of Sale, based on estimated future claims. This follows the Matching Principle — the expense is recognized in the same period as the related revenue.

1-24.q: A service-type warranty (extended warranty sold separately) is treated as:
o: An expense at time of sale — same as assurance warranty
A Separate Performance Obligation — revenue deferred and recognized over warranty period
Recognized entirely when the warranty is sold
A contingent liability
1-24.e: Service-Type warranty: treated as a Separate Performance Obligation under ASC 606. The transaction price is Allocated to the warranty. Revenue for the warranty is Deferred and recognized over the Warranty Period as the service is delivered.

1-24.q: Products sold: 500 units. Estimated 3% require warranty service at average cost $50. Warranty expense for the period =
1-24.e: Warranty expense = 500 × 3% × $50 = 500 × 0.03 × $50 = 15 × $50 = $750. This amount is both the warranty expense (IS) and the warranty liability (BS) accrued at time of sale.

1-24.q: When a Warranty Claim is honored (parts and labor provided), the journal entry is:
o: Dr. Warranty Expense / Cr. Cash
Dr. Warranty Liability / Cr. Cash or Inventory (parts used)
Dr. Cash / Cr. Warranty Revenue
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Warranty Liability
1-24.e: When a claim is honored: Debit Warranty Liability (reducing the previously established liability) and Credit Cash or Inventory/Wages Payable (for parts and labor used). No new expense is recorded — it was expensed when the sale was made.

1-24.q: The Key distinction between assurance-type and service-type warranty is whether:
o: The warranty period exceeds one year
The warranty covers manufacturing defects vs. customer damage
The customer can Buy The Warranty Separately — indicating it provides additional value beyond product assurance
The warranty cost is material
1-24.e: Key question: Can the customer Buy the warranty separately or is it always bundled? If separate purchase is possible, it provides Value Beyond basic assurance → service-type → separate performance obligation. If always bundled and just assures basic functionality → assurance-type.

1-24.q: Beginning warranty liability $30,000. New accrual $40,000. Claims paid $35,000. Ending warranty liability =
Extended warranty sold separately for $200 covering 2 years. Year 1 revenue recognized =
o: $100 — straight-line over 2 years
$0 until claims made
$200 minus claims
1-24.e: Service-type warranty = separate performance obligation. Recognized Over Time — straight-line. Year 1 = $200 ÷ 2 = $100.

1-24.q: A product comes with a standard 1-year assurance warranty. An Optional 2-year extended warranty is sold for an extra fee. The extended warranty is:
o: Combined with assurance warranty
A Separate performance obligation — deferred over 2 years
Immediately expensed
Contingent liability only
1-24.e: Optional extended warranty at extra cost = Separate Performance Obligation under ASC 606. Allocate and defer; recognize over 2-year service period.

1-24.q: If estimated warranty costs Increase after initial accrual, the company should:
o: Restate prior periods
Record additional warranty expense Currently
Reduce liability and record gain
Disclose only
1-24.e: Warranty estimate changes = changes in Accounting Estimates — prospective. Record additional expense in current period. No restatement.

1-24.q: The journal entry to Accrue warranty expense is:
o: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Cash
Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Warranty Expense
Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Warranty Liability
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Warranty Liability
1-24.e: Accrual: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Warranty Liability. When claims Paid: Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Cash/Inventory.

1-24.q: 500 units sold at $40 warranty repair cost each, 4% failure rate. Warranty expense =
Warranty Liability on the balance sheet is split into:
o: Long-term only
Current only
Current (next 12 months) and long-term (beyond 12 months)
Off-balance-sheet — notes only
1-24.e: Split: expected claims within 12 months = Current; beyond 12 months = Long-Term.

1-24.q: A Product Recall differs from a warranty claim because:
o: Recalls are never accrued
Recall is accrued when Probable and Estimable — often before individual claims
Warranties are always larger
Recalls are only expensed when paid
1-24.e: Product recalls = contingent liability (ASC 450). Accrue when Probable and Reasonably Estimable — often before specific claims are filed.

1-24.q: Under ASC 606, the key question for classifying a bundled warranty is whether:
o: Warranty period exceeds 12 months
Customer can purchase it separately
Warranty Provides Additional Service beyond assurance
Historical claim data exists
1-24.e: If warranty provides Additional Service beyond assuring product meets specifications → service-type (separate Po). If only assures compliance → assurance-type → ASC 460 accrual.

1-24.q: Service-type warranty revenue is recognized:
o: At point of sale
Over The Coverage Period (typically straight-line)
Only when claims are filed
When warranty expires
1-24.e: Service-type warranty revenue recognized Over Time — straight-line over coverage period unless another pattern better reflects service delivery.

1-24.q: Assurance-type warranty in a product sale: All revenue is recognized at delivery because:
o: Warranty is immaterial
Warranty is Not a separate performance obligation — accounted for under ASC 460
Customer chose not to pay separately
All warranties are point-in-time
1-24.e: Assurance-type warranties are Not separate POs under ASC 606. All revenue recognized at delivery. Warranty obligation then accrued as expense under ASC 460.

1-24.q: When a third party provides warranty coverage, the manufacturer:
o: Has no obligation — insurer takes all risk
May Still have net warranty obligation if it is the primary obligor
Records insurance premium as warranty expense
Records a warranty asset
1-24.e: If manufacturer is the Primary Obligor to customers, it still records the warranty liability. Substance of the arrangement determines accounting.

1-24.q: Warranty expense on the income statement is typically presented as:
o: Revenue deduction
Financing cost
Operating Expense (COGS or selling expense)
Extraordinary item
1-24.e: Warranty expense = Operating Expense — typically in COGS (manufacturers) or Selling Expense. It is a cost of the obligation created at the time of sale.

1-24.q: Changes in warranty Estimates affect:
o: Prior period retained earnings
Current And Future Periods — prospective treatment
Both current and prior periods proportionally
Only future periods — never current
1-24.e: Estimate changes = prospective. Adjust Current and Future periods for the revised expectation. No restatement of prior periods.

1-24.q: Warranty claims Paid are recorded as:
o: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Cash
Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Cash (or Parts Inventory)
Dr Apic, Cr Cash
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Cash
1-24.e: When claims are honored: Dr Warranty Liability (reduces the balance), Cr Cash (or Inventory/AP for parts). This settles the previously accrued obligation.

1-25.q: Which one of the following temporary differences will result in a deferred tax asset?
o: Unrealized (holding) gains on equity securities recognized in financial income but not taxable until the securities are sold.
Investment gains on an equity investment accounted for under the equity method in financial income and under the cost less impairment method for income tax purposes. q
Use of the straight-line depreciation method in financial income and the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) on the income tax return.
Advance rental receipts reported on the tax return as revenue when received but recognized as revenue in financial income over the term of the rental agreement.
1-25.e: A deferred tax asset arises as a result of either: 1) a revenue that is reported in taxable income before it is recognized in financial income, or 2) an expense that is recognized in financial income before it is deductible for tax purposes. Because these are advance rental receipts, they will not be recognized in financial income until a later period, but they will be taxable when received. This is revenue reported in taxable income before it is recognized in financial income.
wrongWhy: Though recognition in financial income of unrealized gains on equity securities while not recognizing a taxable gain, if any, until the securities are sold will cause a temporary difference, this difference will lead to a deferred tax liability, not a deferred tax asset.

Though the use of the different methods of accounting for investments will cause a temporary difference, this difference will lead to a deferred tax liability, not a deferred tax asset. This is because the revenue will be recognized first on the financial statements under the equity method while for tax purposes, it will be reported later when a dividend is distributed, subject to the dividends received deduction.

Though the use of an accelerated depreciation method on the income tax return and the use of the straight-line method of depreciation in financial income will cause a temporary difference, this difference will lead to a deferred tax liability in the early years of the asset's life, not a deferred tax asset. Ci

1-25.q: A tax rate other than the current tax rate may be used to calculate the deferred income tax amount on the statement of financial position if a(n) a e A. election has been made to apply past tax rates.
o: election has been made to apply past tax rates.
net operating loss carryback exists.
future tax rate change is considered more likely than not to occur.
future tax rate has been enacted into law.
1-25.e: If the future tax rate has actually been codified into law and it will be the effective tax rate when the deferred tax asset or liability reverses, then that future tax rate should be used to calculate the amount of the deferred tax asset or liability. If a future tax rate change is merely being considered, the current tax rate should be used to calculate the amount of the deferred tax asset or liability.
wrongWhy: A business does not have the liberty to decide what tax rate will be applicable to its taxable income, either present or future. It must use the enacted tax rates that have been codified into law.

If a net operating loss carryback exists and the company had taxable income during the past one or two taxable years, the company can request a refund of taxes already paid. That will have no effect on deferred taxes or the tax rate used to calculate the deferred income tax amount on the statement of financial position.

If the future tax rate has actually been codified into law and it will be the effective tax rate when the deferred tax asset or liability reverses, then that future tax rate should be used to calculate the amount of the deferred tax asset or liability. However, if a future tax rate change is merely considered more likely than not to occur, the current tax rate should be used to calculate the amount of the deferred tax asset or liability. Ci

1-25.q: The purpose of interperiod income tax allocation is to
o: provide proper disclosure of a distribution of earnings to a taxing authority.
recognize a tax asset or liability for the tax consequences of temporary differences that exist at the date of the balance sheet.
adjust the income tax expense on the income statement to be consistent with the income tax liability shown on the balance sheet.
reconcile the tax consequences of permanent and temporary differences that appear on the company’s current financial statements.
1-25.e: The purpose of interperiod income tax allocation is to allocate income tax expense to the correct period in the financial statements. The effect of interperiod income tax allocation is to recognize a deferred tax asset or deferred tax liability on the company’s accrual-based financial statements to recognize the future tax consequences of events that represent temporary differences between financial income and taxable income that exist at the financial statement date.
wrongWhy: The purpose of interperiod income tax allocation is not to provide proper disclosure of a distribution of earnings to a taxing authority. It is to allocate income tax expense to the correct period in the financial statements. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The purpose of interperiod income tax allocation is not to adjust the income tax expense on the income statement to be consistent with the income tax liability shown on the balance sheet. It is to allocate income tax expense to the correct period in the financial statements. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The purpose of interperiod income tax allocation is not to reconcile the tax consequences of permanent and temporary differences that appear on the company’s current financial statements. It is to allocate income tax expense to the correct period in the financial statements. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Ci

1-25.q: For a corporation that is subject to the U.S. Income Tax Code and reports its financial results in accordance with U.S. GAAP, which one of the following requires the recognition of a deferred tax liability?
o: Interest received on state and municipal bonds.
During the early years of an asset's life, use of an accelerated depreciation method for tax purposes while using straight-line depreciation for financial income purposes.
The first and last months' rental revenue on a lease received in advance when the lessee signs the lease.
An accrual for product warranty expenses that will be incurred and paid in the future.
1-25.e: Deferred tax liabilities arise when financial net income is higher than taxable net income for a period and that difference is expected to reverse in the future. Using an accelerated depreciation method for tax purposes while using straight-line depreciation for financial income purposes will cause tax-deductible depreciation to be higher in the early years of an asset's life than financial depreciation expense, and thus financial net income will be higher than taxable net income during those years. As a result, income taxes paid will be lower in the early years of the asset's life, leading to a deferred tax liability. The liability will reverse in the later years of the asset's life because then, depreciation expense reported on the tax return will be lower and taxable income and income tax due will be higher than they will be for financial income.
wrongWhy: Deferred tax liabilities arise when financial income is higher than taxable income for a period and that difference is expected to reverse in the future. Interest received on state and municipal bonds is not taxable and therefore creates a permanent difference. Permanent differences do not give rise to either deferred tax assets or deferred tax liabilities.

Deferred tax liabilities arise when financial income is higher than taxable income for a period and that difference is expected to reverse in the future. Rental revenue received in advance leads to taxable income being higher than financial income and the creation of a deferred tax asset, not a deferred tax liability.

Deferred tax liabilities arise when financial income is higher than taxable income for a period and that difference is expected to reverse in the future. An accrual for warranty expenses in financial income that will be paid in the future cannot be deducted on the tax return until the expenses are actually paid. Therefore, the accrual leads to taxable income being higher than financial income and the creation of a deferred tax asset, not a deferred tax liability. Ci /2

1-25.q: Temporary and permanent differences between taxable income and pre-tax financial income differ in that:
o: Temporary differences include items that enter into pre-tax financial income but never into taxable income.
Only temporary differences have deferred tax consequences.
Only permanent differences have deferred tax consequences. a
Temporary differences do not give rise to future taxable or deductible amounts.
1-25.e: Only temporary differences will give rise to deferred tax items.
wrongWhy: Items that enter financial income but will never enter taxable income are permanent differences, not temporary differences, and they do not give rise to deferred taxes.
Permanent differences do not give rise to deferred tax amounts.
Temporary differences do give rise to future taxable or deductible amounts. https:thwmw. ton A Pe Sana, Fdemal Financial Reporting 330Q 7 1/1

1-25.q: Which one of the following statements describes the asset-liability method of accounting for deferred income taxes?
o: The tax effects of temporary differences are not reported separately but are reported as adjustments to the amounts of specific assets and liabilities and the related revenues and expenses. a
The appropriate tax rate to be reported on the income statement is the tax actually levied in that year, meaning no deferred taxes would be reported.
The amount of deferred income tax is based on the tax rates expected to be in ffect during the periods in which the temporary differences reverse.
The amount of deferred income tax is based on tax rates in effect when temporary differences originate.
1-25.e: By definition this is the tax rate that is used in the calculation of deferred taxes. The amount of the deferred tax asset or liability is the amount of the temporary difference multiplied by the nacted tax rate that will be in force for the period in which the deferred tax asset or liability is xpected to reverse. Whether the deferred tax is a deferred tax asset or liability depends upon the nature of the temporary difference.
wrongWhy: The tax effects of temporary differences are reported separately from the items to which they relate.

Deferred taxes are calculated using the tax rate that is expected to be in effect in the time period in which the temporary difference will reverse. Because this rate is usually different from the current period tax rate, there are deferred tax assets and liabilities and they do need to be reported.
The amount of deferred tax is not based on the tax rate in effect when temporary differences originate. Ci /2

1-25.q: This data pertains to Lally Corporation for 20X4 and 20X5. X5 20X4 Income before income taxes $5,000,000 $4,000,000 Interest income included above that was not subject to income taxes 100,000 100,000 Income before income taxes in 20X4 included a gain of $80,000 for the sale of real property, for which Lally financed the sale on a short-term note. Lally received full payment on the note from the buyer in two installments during 20X85, reported the gain on its 20X5 income tax return as an installment sale, and paid the tax on it. Lally was subject to an effective income tax rate of 40% in 20X4 and 20X5. The deferred tax asset or liability reported on Lally Corporation's statement of financial position on December 31, X5 is
1-25.e: In this question there is only one temporary difference: the gain on the sale of the real property that was reported in financial income in 20X4 but received and reported on the 20X5 income tax return as installment sale income. The gain in 20X4 created a deferred tax liability for Lally for 20X4. However, during 20X5, full payment from the buyer was received and the installment sale was reported on Lally's 20X5 tax return. Lally paid the tax due on the gain in 20X5, so the deferred tax liability fully reversed in 20X5. Thus, there is no deferred tax asset or liability at the end of 20X5. The interest income that was not subject to income taxes is a permanent difference. Permanent differences do not give rise to deferred tax assets or liabilities.
wrongWhy: This is the $100,000 interest income received each year minus the $80,000 gain on the sale of real property earned in X4, the difference of $20,000 multiplied by the 40% tax rate. The interest income that was not subject to income taxes is a permanent difference. Permanent differences do not give rise to deferred tax assets or liabilities. The gain on the sale of the real property that was reported as financial income in 20X4 but reported on the 20X5 tax return as installment sale income created a deferred tax liability for 20X4, but during 20X5, the full payment was received from the buyer so the tax was paid and the deferred tax liability fully reversed.

This answer treats the interest income as a temporary difference, which it is not. The interest revenue on tax-exempt securities was a permanent difference that did not give rise to any deferred tax liability.
This is the deferred tax liability at the end of 20X4. PART SERTION A. Femna Enansal agporing 220 Q 9 Ci /2

1-25.q: Bearings Manufacturing Company Inc. purchased a new machine on January 1, 20X0 for $100,000. The company uses the straight-line depreciation method with an estimated equipment life of 5 years and a zero salvage value for financial statement purposes, and uses the 3-year Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) and the half-year convention with an estimated equipment life of 3 years for income tax reporting purposes. Bearings is subject to a 35% marginal income tax rate. Assume that the deferred tax liability at the beginning of the year is zero and that Bearings has a positive earnings tax position. The MACRS depreciation rates for 3-year equipment are shown below. Year Rate .33% .45% .81% TA1% What is the deferred tax liability at December 31, 20X0 (rounded to the nearest whole dollar)?
1-25.e: This question is made easier for us by the fact that the tax rate remains 35% throughout all of the years of the asset's life. In order to calculate the deferred tax liability at the end of 20X0 we simply need to determine the difference between the book depreciation and the tax depreciation for 20X0 and multiply it by the 35% tax rate. The book depreciation is straight-line with no salvage value, and with a cost of $100,000 and useful life of 5 years, the book depreciation is $20,000 per year. For tax purposes, 33.33% of the asset's cost (or $33,333) will be depreciated in the first year. The difference between the two methods is $13,333 and this is what needs to be multiplied by 35%. This gives us a deferred tax liability of $4,666. If the tax rate had changed in future periods the calculation would have been more involved than it is in this problem.
wrongWhy: This is the tax effect of the $20,000 of book depreciation.
This is the tax deductible depreciation expense for the first year.
This is the tax effect of the tax deductible depreciation from the first year. i r Ci /2

1-25.q: A liability that represents the accumulated difference between the income tax expense reported on the firm’s books and the income tax actually paid is:
o: Taxes payable.
Deferred taxes.
Value-added taxes.
Capital gains tax.
1-25.e: Deferred tax liability represents the accumulated difference between the income tax xpense reported on the firm’s books and the income tax actually paid.
wrongWhy: This is not the definition of taxes payable.
This is not the definition of value-added taxes.
This is not the definition of capital gains tax. https:thwmw. ton A Pe Sana, Fdemal Financial Reporting 330Q M4 1/1

1-26.q: Under ASC 842, All leases (with limited exceptions) are now:
o: Kept off-balance-sheet for operating leases
Recognized On The Balance Sheet as a Right-of-Use (Rou) asset and lease liability
Only recognized if they transfer ownership
Expensed immediately when signed
1-26.e: ASC 842 (effective 2019) Requires all leases (operating and finance, with exceptions for short-term leases < 12 months) to be Recognized On The Balance Sheet as a Rou Asset and Lease Liability. This was the major change — previously operating leases were off-balance-sheet.

1-26.q: A Finance Lease results in which income statement presentation?
o: A single straight-line lease expense
Separate amortization expense And interest expense — total expense front-loaded
Lease expense recognized only when cash is paid
No income statement effect
1-26.e: Finance Lease: income statement shows Amortization of the Rou asset (usually straight-line) And Interest on the lease liability (higher in early periods). Total expense is Front-Loaded — higher in early years of the lease compared to a straight-line operating lease.

1-26.q: The Lease Liability is measured at:
o: Total undiscounted lease payments
Present Value of future lease payments
Historical cost of the leased asset
Fair value of the asset at commencement
1-26.e: Lease liability = Present Value of future lease payments, discounted at the rate implicit in the lease (or if not determinable, the lessee's incremental borrowing rate). Future payments are discounted to reflect the time value of money.

1-26.q: An operating lease requires how many expense lines on the income statement?
o: Three — rent expense, interest, amortization
Two — amortization and interest
One — a single lease expense recognized on a straight-line basis
None — operating leases are off-balance-sheet
1-26.e: Operating Lease (ASC 842): a Single Lease Expense recognized on a Straight-Line basis over the lease term. Even though there is a Rou asset and liability on the balance sheet (like finance lease), the income statement shows only One straight-line expense — a key difference from finance lease treatment.

1-26.q: The Right-of-Use (Rou) Asset is initially measured at approximately:
o: Historical cost of the asset
Gross lease payments over the term
Present Value of lease payments + initial direct costs + prepaid rent
Fair value of the leased asset
1-26.e: Rou Asset initially = Lease Liability (PV of payments) + initial direct costs + any prepaid lease payments + initial estimate of Aro. Essentially, it approximates the PV of the lease payments. As payments are made, both the Rou asset and lease liability decrease.

1-26.q: A lease is classified as a Finance lease if:
o: It is for less than 12 months
The lessee always intends to purchase the asset
It meets Any One of five specified criteria (e.g., ownership transfer, purchase option, 75% life, 90% FV, specialized asset)
The lessor chooses to classify it as finance
1-26.e: Finance lease: if the lease meets Any One of five criteria: (1) title transfers, (2) purchase option likely to be exercised, (3) lease term ≥ 75% of economic life, (4) PV of payments ≥ 90% of asset FV, (5) specialized asset with no alternative use.

1-26.q: Short-Term Lease Exemption under ASC 842 applies to leases with term of:
o: Less than 24 months
12 months or less, no purchase option reasonably certain to exercise
Less than 36 months
Any lease under $10,000
1-26.e: Short-term exemption: term ≤ 12 months And no purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise. Payments expensed straight-line. No Rou asset or lease liability recognized.

1-26.q: Under IFRS 16, Low-Value Asset exemption applies when:
o: Annual payment below $1,000
Asset's fair value when New is low (e.g., ~$5,000 threshold)
Lease term under 6 months
Lessee has a purchase option
1-26.e: IFRS 16 low-value exemption based on the Underlying Asset'S Value when new (not lease term). IASB used ~$5,000 in basis for conclusions. E.g., tablets, small furniture.

1-26.q: Lease Modification that grants Additional right of use at standalone price is treated as:
o: Remeasurement of existing lease
Separate New Lease
Error correction
Operating expense
1-26.e: Modification = separate new lease when: (1) additional right of use Not in original lease, And (2) price increase reflects standalone price.

1-26.q: Lease Incentives paid by lessor to lessee Reduce the lessee's:
o: Lease liability
Lease expense immediately
Rou Asset initial measurement
Retained earnings
1-26.e: Lease incentives received reduce the initial Rou Asset measurement. Lower Rou asset → lower amortization → lower expense over lease term.

1-26.q: Initial Direct Costs incurred by the lessee to obtain a lease are:
o: Expensed immediately
Added to initial Rou Asset measurement
Deducted from lease liability
Recorded as separate prepaid
1-26.e: Initial direct costs (commissions, fees to obtain lease) added to the Initial Measurement of the Rou Asset. Amortized as part of lease expense or amortization expense.

1-26.q: In Sale-Leaseback where transfer qualifies as a Sale, the seller-lessee:
o: Recognizes no gain or loss
Recognizes gain/loss on sale And records leaseback as new lease
Records financing liability for full proceeds
Continues to carry asset on balance sheet
1-26.e: Qualifying sale-leaseback: derecognize asset, recognize Gain or Loss on sale (related to rights transferred), account for Leaseback as separate operating or finance lease.

1-26.q: Key income statement difference between Operating and Finance Lease (lessee):
o: Finance leases cost more over lifetime
Operating: single lease expense; Finance: amortization + interest (front-loaded)
Operating has higher interest
Finance shows no depreciation
1-26.e: Operating = One Line (straight-line expense). Finance = Two Lines (amortization + interest — higher in early years). Finance lease expense is front-loaded.

1-26.q: Variable Lease Payments based on usage are:
o: Included in lease liability at commencement
Excluded from liability — expensed when incurred
Capitalized in Rou asset
Contingent liabilities
1-26.e: Usage-based variable payments cannot be reliably estimated → Excluded from lease liability. Expensed as Incurred when usage occurs.

1-26.q: Lease Term includes:
o: Non-cancellable period only
Non-cancellable + optional periods the lessee is Reasonably Certain to exercise
Full legal term regardless of options
Only periods with fixed payments
1-26.e: Lease term = non-cancellable period + optional renewal periods where lessee is Reasonably Certain to exercise. High threshold — similar to 'reasonably assured' under old GAAP.

1-26.q: For a Finance Lease, interest expense each period =
o: Fixed payment ÷ remaining term
Lease Liability Balance × discount rate (effective interest method)
Straight-line over lease term
Fixed payment × implicit rate
1-26.e: Finance lease interest = Carrying Value of lease liability × discount rate. Effective interest method — higher in early periods (larger balance), decreasing over time.

1-26.q: For the Lessor, an Operating Lease means:
o: Derecognize asset, record receivable
Keep asset on balance sheet, recognize rental income straight-line
Record finance receivable at PV
Recognize profit at commencement
1-26.e: Lessor operating lease: Keeps asset (continues depreciation), recognizes Rental Income straight-line over lease term. Asset returned at end.

1-26.q: A Sales-Type Lease (lessor) results in:
o: Rental income straight-line
Derecognize asset; record net investment; recognize selling profit at commencement + interest income over term
Keep asset at original cost
No income until payments received
1-26.e: Sales-type: derecognize asset, record net investment (PV of future payments), recognize Selling Profit at commencement and Interest Income over the lease term.

1-26.q: For Lessee operating lease, the Year 1 journal entry is:
o: Dr Rou amortization, Cr Rou Asset + Dr Interest, Cr Liability
Dr Lease Expense (single line), Cr Cash/Lease Liability (adjust Rou and liability)
Dr Depreciation, Cr Acc Depr + Dr Interest, Cr Cash
Dr Rou Asset, Cr Lease Liability
1-26.e: Operating lease: Single Lease Expense line (straight-line). Debit Lease Expense, credit Cash (and adjust Rou asset and liability per amortization tables). One income statement line.

1-26.q: Finance lease Rou asset amortization period: if ownership transfers Or purchase option reasonably certain —
o: Lease term only
Useful Life of the underlying asset
Shorter of useful life or lease term
Longer of useful life or lease term
1-26.e: If ownership transfers/purchase option certain → amortize over Useful Life (same as owned asset). If neither → shorter of useful life or lease term.

1-27.q: When a cash dividend is Declared, the journal entry is:
o: Dr. Cash / Cr. Retained Earnings
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Dividends Payable
Dr. Dividends Payable / Cr. Cash
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Cash
1-27.e: Declaration creates the liability: Dr. Retained Earnings (or Dividends Declared), Cr. Dividends Payable. The Payment date entry is: Dr. Dividends Payable, Cr. Cash. No journal entry is required on the Record Date.

1-27.q: A Small stock dividend (<25%) is recorded at:
o: Par value per share
Zero — only memo entry
Fair Value per share — transferred from Re to Apic
Cost of treasury shares
1-27.e: Small stock dividend: recorded at Fair Value of the shares issued. Dr. Retained Earnings (FV), Cr. Common Stock (par value × new shares), Cr. Apic (excess above par). Total equity is Unchanged — just a reclassification within equity.

1-27.q: Total shareholders' equity is affected by which of the following?
o: Declaring a stock dividend
Issuing stock for cash (Increases equity)
Paying a previously declared cash dividend (Div Payable → Cash)
Converting treasury stock between equity accounts
1-27.e: Issuing Stock for cash Increases total equity (cash in, equity account up). Stock dividends just reclassify within equity (no total change). Paying a declared dividend reduces cash and removes the payable (net effect = zero on total equity since Re was reduced at declaration). Treasury stock conversions are reclassifications.

1-27.q: A material accounting error from a prior period is corrected as a:
o: Current period expense on the income statement
Prior Period Adjustment to beginning Retained Earnings — comparative FS are restated
Extraordinary item
Change in accounting estimate
1-27.e: Prior Period Error Correction: adjusted against the Beginning Retained Earnings balance for the earliest period presented. Prior period FS are Restated. Not reported on the current income statement (would distort current results). Disclosures required.

1-27.q: Accumulated OCI (Aoci) in equity includes:
o: Net income from the current period
Dividends paid
Unrealized gains on AFS securities, Fx translation adjustments, pension adjustments
Retained earnings balance
1-27.e: Aoci accumulates OCI items that bypass the income statement: unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, foreign currency Translation adjustments, pension liability adjustments, and effective portion of cash flow hedges. Aoci is a separate equity component.

1-27.q: Retained Earnings can be Reduced by all of the following Except:
o: Cash dividends declared
Net loss for the period
Prior period error correction (overstatement of prior income)
Issuing new common stock
1-27.e: Issuing new common stock Increases equity (increases Common Stock and Apic). Retained Earnings is reduced by: dividends declared, net losses, and prior period adjustments for overstatement of prior income.

1-27.q: A Large stock dividend (25%+) is recorded at:
o: Fair market value
Par Value per share
Book value per share
No value — no entry
1-27.e: Large stock dividends (≥25%) recorded at Par Value. Dr Retained Earnings (par × new shares), Cr Common Stock. Small dividends (<25%) recorded at Fair Market Value.

1-27.q: A Property Dividend is recorded at:
o: Book value of property
Fair Value at declaration date — with gain/loss on revaluation
Cost minus depreciation
Par value equivalent
1-27.e: Property remeasured to Fair Value first (gain/loss recognized), then dividend declared at fair value. Dr Retained Earnings (FV), Cr Dividends Payable. On payment: Dr Dividends Payable, Cr Asset.

1-27.q: A Liquidating Dividend returns:
o: Company stock to shareholders
Paid-In Capital (not earnings) to shareholders
Excess cash beyond retained earnings
More than net income for the year
1-27.e: Liquidating dividend returns Invested Capital — debited to Apic, not Retained Earnings. Signals return of capital rather than distribution of profits.

1-27.q: A stock dividend's effect on Total Stockholders' Equity is:
o: Increases total equity
Decreases total equity
No Effect — reclassification within equity (Re to Common Stock/Apic)
Decreases equity by par value of new shares
1-27.e: Stock dividend = Reclassification: Re decreases; Common Stock (and Apic for small dividends) increases. Total equity Unchanged. No assets leave the company.

1-27.q: Appropriated (restricted) Retained Earnings means:
o: Re invested in fixed assets
Portion restricted for specific purpose — Unavailable for dividends
Re reduced by prior period errors
Re belonging to Nci
1-27.e: Appropriated Re = voluntary/required restriction unavailable for distribution. Signals a portion is set aside (e.g., loan covenant, expansion). Total equity amount unchanged.

1-27.q: A Prior Period Error correction is reported as:
o: Current period income adjustment
Adjustment to Beginning Retained Earnings of earliest period (restatement)
OCI component
Notes only
1-27.e: Prior period errors (mathematical mistakes, GAAP misapplication) = Restatement. Restate prior periods and adjust Beginning Re of earliest period presented.

1-27.q: A voluntary Change In Accounting Principle is reported:
o: Prospectively
Cumulative-effect in current income
Retrospectively — restate all prior periods presented
As OCI item
1-27.e: Accounting principle change = Retrospective Application. Restate prior periods as if new principle always used. Adjust beginning Re of earliest period.

1-27.q: Comprehensive Income equals:
o: Net income only
Net income Plus Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)
Operating income plus OCI
Total revenues minus expenses
1-27.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI. OCI includes: unrealized AFS gains/losses, pension adjustments, foreign currency translation, effective cash flow hedge amounts.

1-27.q: Statement of Shareholders' Equity shows changes in all Except:
o: Retained earnings
Common stock and Apic
Accumulated OCI
Cash Flows From Operations
1-27.e: Statement of Se shows changes in all Equity components. Cash flows belong to the Statement Of Cash Flows, not the equity statement.

1-27.q: Book Value Per Common Share =
o: Total assets ÷ total outstanding
(Total equity − Preferred liquidation value) ÷ Common Shares Outstanding
Net income ÷ common shares
Total equity ÷ authorized shares
1-27.e: Book value per common share = (Total equity − Preferred liquidation value) ÷ Common shares outstanding.

1-27.q: Return On Equity (ROE) =
o: Net income ÷ total assets
Net income ÷ Average Total Stockholders' Equity
Operating income ÷ common equity
Gross profit ÷ average equity
1-27.e: ROE = Net Income ÷ Average Total Se. Measures efficiency in using shareholders' capital to generate profit.

1-27.q: Dividends on Non-Cumulative preferred stock Not declared in a year:
o: Accumulate for future years
Lost permanently
Convert to common dividends
Accrued as liability
1-27.e: Non-cumulative: skipped dividends are Lost permanently. Do not accumulate or carry forward.

1-27.q: Accumulated OCI (Aoci) includes:
o: Net income for the year
Cash dividends declared
Unrealized AFS debt gains/losses, pension adjustments, foreign currency translation
Treasury stock purchases
1-27.e: Aoci = OCI items that Bypass the income statement: unrealized AFS debt securities, pension liability adjustments, foreign currency translation adjustments, effective cash flow hedge amounts.

1-28.q: Astock dividend
o: increases shareholders' wealth.
increases the debt-to-equity ratio of a firm.
decreases the size of the firm.
decreases future earnings per share.
1-28.e: In a stock dividend more shares are issued to existing shareholders. Since there is no increase in income from this event but there are more shares outstanding, future arnings per share will decrease as a result of the stock dividend.
wrongWhy: A stock dividend does not in itself increase shareholder wealth. The stock dividend provides more shares to each shareholder, but since the value of each share decreases, the total value of the shares remains unchanged.
A stock dividend has no impact on the company's liabilities or the book value of the company's total equity. Therefore, there is no effect on the firm's debt-to-equity ratio.
A stock dividend does not impact the size of the firm.

1-28.q: Stock dividends and stock splits differ in that
o: Ina stock split, a larger number of new shares replaces the outstanding shares.
Stock splits involve a bookkeeping transfer from retained earnings to the capital stock account.
Stock splits are paid in additional shares of common stock, whereas a stock dividend results in replacement of all outstanding shares with a new issue of shares.
Astock dividend results in a decline in the par value per share.
1-28.e: In a stock split, there is no journal entry. However, the result is that a larger number of shares replaces the existing shares that are outstanding.
wrongWhy: Stock splits do not require a journal entry. Stock dividends involve a transfer from retained earnings to the common stock account.
This answer reverses the effect of stock splits and stock dividends.
It is in a stock split that the par value of the shares is reduced. te: art-

1-28.q: The policy decision that by itself is least likely to affect the value of the firm is the
o: Investment in a project with a large net present value.
Sale of a risky division that will now increase the credit rating of the entire company.
Use of a more highly leveraged capital structure that resulted in a lower cost of capital.
Distribution of stock dividends to shareholders.
1-28.e: A stock dividend should not affect the value of the company. The distribution of a stock dividend does not increase or decrease equity and will not generate a profit or cause a loss.
wrongWhy: If the company enters into a project that has a large net present value, this should increase the value of the company.
If the company sells a risky division and improves its credit rating, this should increase the value of the company.
A structure that results in a lower cost of capital should increase the value of a firm. te: art-

1-28.q: A publicly-traded corporation issues 10,000 shares of new common stock for $50 per share. The common stock has a par value of $5 per share. Which one of the following statements is correct?
o: Cash increases by $450,000; common stock increases by $50,000, and additional paid-in capital increases by $500,000.
Cash increases by $500,000, and common stock increases by $500,000.
Additional paid-in capital increases by $500,000; no other accounts are affected.
Cash increases by $500,000; common stock increases by $50,000; and additional paid-in capital increase by $450,000.
1-28.e: When common shares are issued for cash, the journal entry for the issuance of common shares is: D OF- eee cash received Cr Common Shates.............:ccceeeeceeeeeeeeee par value of shares issued Cr Additional paid-in capital — common shares.... balancing amount The amount debited to cash is the $500,000 received for the sale of the stock. The amount credited to common shares is the $5 par value per share multiplied by the 10,000 shares issued, or $50,000. The amount credited to additional paid-in capital is the difference, or $450,000.
wrongWhy: Cssh does not increase by $450,000, and additional paid-in capital does not increase by $500,000. Please see the correct answer for more information.
Common stock does not increase by $500,000; another account is affected as well. Please see the correct answer for more information.
Additional paid-in capital does not increase by $500,000, and other accounts are affected. Please see the correct answer for more information. Ci /2

1-28.q: On December 15, a company distributed a cash dividend of $120,000 and declared a 5% stock dividend with a market value of $100,000. If the company uses U.S. GAAP, these two transactions would decrease the company’s total shareholders’ equity by
1-28.e: Neither the distribution of a cash dividend nor the declaration of a stock dividend will decrease the company's total shareholders’ equity. The distribution, that is, the payment of a cash dividend does not reduce total shareholders’ quity. Retained earnings, an equity account, is reduced when a cash dividend is declared. Retained earnings is debited (which reduces it) and dividends payable, a current liability, is credited on the date of the dividend's declaration; and on the date of payment (that is, distribution), dividends payable is debited to reduce it and cash is credited. The declaration of a stock dividend transfers funds among accounts within the equity section of the balance sheet but it does not reduce total shareholders’ equity.
wrongWhy: Neither the distribution of a cash dividend nor the declaration of a stock dividend will decrease the company's total shareholders’ equity. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the amount of the cash dividend that was distributed, or paid. The distribution, that is, the payment of a cash dividend does not reduce total shareholders' equity. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the market value of the stock dividend. The declaration of a stock dividend transfers funds among accounts within the equity section of the balance sheet but it does not reduce total shareholders’ equity. 1-28.q: The par value of common stock represents
o: The liability ceiling of a shareholder when a company undergoes bankruptcy proceedings.
The amount that must be recorded on the issuing corporation's record as paid-in capital.
The estimated fair value of the stock when it was issued.
The total value of the stock that must be entered in the issuing corporation's records.
1-28.e: The par value of a share is the amount of the legal capital that share represents. It also represents the maximum liability of a shareholder when the company goes through bankruptcy. Many companies have shares with a low par value. The amount of the legal capital is also unable to be distributed by the company.
wrongWhy: The amount that is recorded as paid in capital is the difference between the fair value of what was received and the par value of the shares that were issued.
Though the par value may in some cases be equal to the fair value of the stock, this is not what par value represents.

The value that is entered into the company's records for the stock is the fair value of all assets or services received in exchange for the stock. This amount will be split between two accounts, common stock and additional paid-in capital, but the total fair value of what was received is ntered into the company's books. 1-28.q: The board of directors of Markham Corp. met on May 5, 20X5 and declared a quarterly cash dividend in the amount of $800,000 ($0.50 per share). The dividend was paid on May 28, 20X5 to shareholders of record as of May 15, 20X5. outstanding shares will decrease.

A small stock dividend is accounted for on the date of declaration by debiting (decreasing) retained earnings by the fair value of the shares distributed, crediting (increasing) the common stock account by the par value of the shares distributed, and crediting (increasing) the additional paid-in-capital account by the difference. Thus additional paid-in capital will increase on the date of declaration.

1-28.q: When a company desires to increase the market value per share of its common stock, the company can
o: Split the stock.
Sell preferred stock.
Sell treasury stock.

1-28.e: In a reverse stock split the company reduces the number of shares outstanding. For example, in a 1-for-2 reverse stock split, every two shares that are held by an investor become one share. This one share, however, has a value that is twice as high as an individual share before the reverse stock split. Therefore, a reverse stock split will increase the market value of a common share.
wrongWhy: In a stock split each share is split into an increased number of shares. For example, in a 2-for- split, each share that is held by an investor becomes two shares. However, the market value of each of those shares is half what it had been before the split. A stock split reduces the market value of each common share. It does not increase it.

Selling preferred stock will have only an indirect effect on the market value of the company's common stock, and it would decrease the common stock's market value, not increase it. Preferred stock reduces the income available to common shareholders which in turn reduces basic and diluted earnings per share. The reduction of earnings per share would reduce the value of the common stock in the judgment of the market, so the market value of the common stock could decline. It would not increase.

If the company sells treasury shares there will be more shares outstanding among which the value of the company needs to be divided. A sale of treasury shares will reduce the value of an individual common share. https:thwmw. ton A Pe Sana, Fdemal Financial Reporting 330Q 63 /2
